units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: We survived 1984, and computers did not take over the world, though some people might find that hard to believe. If there's any one individual who can be either blamed or praised for the proliferation of computers, you, the 29-year-old father of the computer revolution, are the prime contender. It has also made you wealthy beyond dreams—your stock was worth almost a half billion dollars at one point, wasn't it?
units.translation_cn: 我们挺过了1984年，计算机没有占领世界，尽管有些人可能觉得这难以置信。如果说有哪个人可以因为计算机的普及而受到指责或赞扬，那么你，这位29岁的计算机革命之父，就是主要竞争者。它也让你富裕得超乎想象——你的股票一度价值近5亿美元，不是吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I actually lost $250,000,000 in one year when the stock went down. [Laughs]
units.translation_cn: 当股票下跌时，我一年内实际上损失了2.5亿美元。[笑]

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You can laugh about it?
units.translation_cn: 你能笑得出来？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I'm not going to let it ruin my life. Isn't it kind of funny? You know, my main reaction to this money thing is that it's humorous, all the attention to it, because it's hardly the most insightful or valuable thing that's happened to me in the past ten years. But it makes me feel old, sometimes, when I speak at a campus and I find that what students are most in awe of is the fact that I'm a millionaire. When I went to school, it was right after the Sixties and before this general wave of practical purposefulness had set in. Now students aren't even thinking in idealistic terms, or at least nowhere near as much. They certainly are not letting any of the philosophical issues of the day take up too much of their time as they study their business majors. The idealistic wind of the Sixties was still at our backs, though, and most of the people I know who are my age have that ingrained in them forever.
units.translation_cn: 我不会让它毁了我的生活。这难道不是有点好笑吗？你知道，我对金钱这件事的主要反应是它很幽默，所有对它的关注，因为它绝不是过去十年里发生在我身上最有洞察力或最有价值的事情。但有时，当我在校园演讲时，它让我觉得自己老了，我发现学生们最敬畏的是我是一个百万富翁的事实。我上学的时候，正好是六十年代之后，在普遍的实用主义浪潮到来之前。现在学生们甚至不再以理想主义的方式思考，或者至少远没有那么多了。他们当然不会让当今的任何哲学问题占据他们太多时间，因为他们正在学习他们的商科专业。然而，六十年代的理想主义之风仍然在我们身后，我认识的大多数同龄人都有这种根深蒂固的东西。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: It's interesting that the computer field has made millionaires of——
units.translation_cn: 有趣的是，计算机领域造就了百万富翁——

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Young maniacs, I know.
units.translation_cn: 年轻的疯子，我知道。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: We were going to say guys like you and Steve Wozniak, working out of a garage only ten years ago. Just what is this revolution you two seem to have started?
units.translation_cn: 我们本来想说像你和史蒂夫·沃兹尼亚克这样的人，十年前还在车库里工作。你们俩似乎发起的这场革命到底是什么？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: We're living in the wake of the petrochemical revolution of 100 years ago. The petrochemical revolution gave us free energy—free mechanical energy, in this case. It changed the texture of society in most ways. This revolution, the information revolution, is a revolution of free energy as well, but of another kind: free intellectual energy. It's very crude today, yet our Macintosh computer takes less power than a 100-watt light bulb to run and it can save you hours a day. What will it be able to do ten or 20 years from now, or 50 years from now? This revolution will dwarf the petrochemical revolution. We're on the forefront.
units.translation_cn: 我们生活在100年前石油化工革命的余波中。石油化工革命给了我们免费的能源——在这种情况下是免费的机械能。它在大多数方面改变了社会的结构。这场革命，信息革命，也是一场免费能源的革命，但却是另一种：免费的智力能源。它今天还很粗糙，但我们的Macintosh电脑运行所需的电力比一个100瓦的灯泡还少，它每天可以为你节省数小时。十年、二十年或五十年后，它将能做什么？这场革命将使石油化工革命相形见绌。我们正处于前沿。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Maybe we should pause and get your definition of what a computer is. How do they work?
units.translation_cn: 也许我们应该暂停一下，听听你对计算机的定义。它们是如何工作的？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Computers are actually pretty simple. We're sitting here on a bench in this cafe [for this part of the Interview]. Let's assume that you understood only the most rudimentary of directions and you asked how to find the rest room. I would have to describe it to you in very specific and precise instructions. I might say, "Scoot sideways two meters off the bench. Stand erect. Lift left foot. Bend left knee until it is horizontal. Extend left foot and shift weight 300 centimeters forward …" and on and on. If you could interpret all those instructions 100 times faster than any other person in this cafe, you would appear to be a magician: You could run over and grab a milk shake and bring it back and set it on the table and snap your fingers, and I'd think you made the milk shake appear, because it was so fast relative to my perception. That's exactly what a computer does. It takes these very, very simple-minded instructions—"Go fetch a number, add it to this number, put the result there, perceive if it's greater than this other number"—but executes them at a rate of, let's say, 1,000,000 per second. At 1,000,000 per second, the results appear to be magic. That's a simple explanation, and the point is that people really don't have to understand how computers work. Most people have no concept of how an automatic transmission works, yet they know how to drive a car. You don't have to study physics to understand the laws of motion to drive a car. You don't have to understand any of this stuff to use Macintosh—but you asked [laughs].
units.translation_cn: 计算机实际上相当简单。我们坐在这个咖啡馆的长凳上[采访的这一部分]。假设你只理解最基本的指示，你问如何找到洗手间。我必须用非常具体和精确的指令向你描述。我可能会说：“从长凳上侧身挪开两米。站直。抬起左脚。弯曲左膝直到水平。伸出左脚并向前移动300厘米……”等等。如果你能比这个咖啡馆里的任何其他人快100倍地解释所有这些指令，你就会看起来像个魔术师：你可以跑过去拿一杯奶昔，把它带回来放在桌子上，然后打个响指，我就会认为你让奶昔出现了，因为它相对于我的感知来说太快了。这正是计算机所做的。它接受这些非常非常简单的指令——“去取一个数字，把它加到这个数字上，把结果放在那里，判断它是否大于另一个数字”——但以每秒，比如说，1,000,000次的速度执行它们。以每秒1,000,000次的速度，结果看起来就像魔术。这是一个简单的解释，关键是人们真的不必理解计算机是如何工作的。大多数人对自动变速器的工作原理一无所知，但他们知道如何开车。你不需要学习物理学来理解运动定律才能开车。你不需要理解这些东西就能使用Macintosh——但你问了[笑]。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Obviously, you believe that computers are going to change our personal lives, but how would you persuade a skeptic? A holdout?
units.translation_cn: 显然，你相信计算机将改变我们的个人生活，但你会如何说服一个怀疑论者？一个顽固分子？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: A computer is the most incredible tool we've ever seen. It can be a writing tool, a communications center, a supercalculator, a planner, a filer and an artistic instrument all in one, just by being given new instructions, or software, to work from. There are no other tools that have the power and versatility of a computer. We have no idea how far it's going to go. Right now, computers make our lives easier. They do work for us in fractions of a second that would take us hours. They increase the quality of life, some of that by simply automating drudgery and some of that by broadening our possibilities. As things progress, they'll be doing more and more for us.
units.translation_cn: 计算机是我们见过的最不可思议的工具。它可以通过被赋予新的指令或软件来工作，集写作工具、通信中心、超级计算器、规划器、文件管理器和艺术工具于一身。没有其他工具拥有计算机的强大功能和多功能性。我们不知道它会发展到什么程度。现在，计算机让我们的生活更轻松。它们在几分之一秒内为我们完成需要数小时的工作。它们提高了生活质量，其中一部分是通过自动化繁琐的工作，另一部分是通过拓宽我们的可能性。随着事情的发展，它们将为我们做越来越多的事情。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How about some concrete reasons to buy a computer today? An executive in your industry recently said, "We've given people computers, but we haven't shown them what to do with them. I can balance my checkbook faster by hand than on my computer." Why should a person buy a computer?
units.translation_cn: 那么，今天购买电脑的一些具体理由是什么？你们行业的一位高管最近说：“我们给了人们电脑，但我们没有告诉他们如何使用它们。我用手平衡我的支票簿比在电脑上更快。”一个人为什么要买电脑？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: There are different answers for different people. In business, that question is easy to answer: You really can prepare documents much faster and at a higher quality level, and you can do many things to increase office productivity. A computer frees people from much of the menial work. Besides that, you are giving them a tool that encourages them to be creative. Remember, computers are tools. Tools help us do our work better. In education, computers are the first thing to come along since books that will sit there and interact with you endlessly, without judgment. Socratic education isn't available anymore, and computers have the potential to be a real breakthrough in the educational process when used in conjunction with enlightened teachers. We're in most schools already.
units.translation_cn: 不同的人有不同的答案。在商业领域，这个问题很容易回答：你确实可以更快、更高质量地准备文档，并且可以做很多事情来提高办公效率。计算机将人们从许多繁琐的工作中解放出来。除此之外，你还给了他们一个鼓励他们发挥创造力的工具。记住，计算机是工具。工具帮助我们更好地完成工作。在教育领域，计算机是自书籍以来第一个能够在那里与你无休止地互动，不带评判的出现的东西。苏格拉底式教育已不复存在，而计算机在与开明的教师结合使用时，有可能成为教育过程中的真正突破。我们已经进入了大多数学校。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Those are arguments for computers in business and in schools, but what about the home?
units.translation_cn: 这些是计算机在商业和学校中的论据，但家庭呢？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: So far, that's more of a conceptual market than a real market. The primary reasons to buy a computer for your home now are that you want to do some business work at home or you want to run educational software for yourself or your children. If you can't justify buying a computer for one of those two reasons, the only other possible reason is that you just want to be computer literate. You know there's something going on, you don't exactly know what it is, so you want to learn. This will change: Computers will be essential in most homes.
units.translation_cn: 到目前为止，这更多是一个概念市场，而不是一个真正的市场。现在为你的家庭购买电脑的主要原因是你想在家做一些商业工作，或者你想为自己或你的孩子运行教育软件。如果你不能因为这两个原因之一而购买电脑，唯一其他可能的原因是你只是想掌握电脑知识。你知道有些事情正在发生，你不知道具体是什么，所以你想学习。这将改变：电脑将在大多数家庭中变得必不可少。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What will change?
units.translation_cn: 什么会改变？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: The most compelling reason for most people to buy a computer for the home will be to link it into a nationwide communications network. We're just in the beginning stages of what will be a truly remarkable breakthrough for most people—as remarkable as the telephone.
units.translation_cn: 大多数人购买家用电脑最令人信服的理由将是将其连接到全国性的通信网络。我们正处于一个对大多数人来说将是真正非凡的突破的初始阶段——就像电话一样非凡。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Specifically, what kind of breakthrough are you talking about?
units.translation_cn: 具体来说，你指的是什么样的突破？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I can only begin to speculate. We see that a lot in our industry: You don't know exactly what's going to result, but you know it's something very big and very good.
units.translation_cn: 我只能开始猜测。我们在行业中看到了很多：你不知道具体会产生什么结果，但你知道它会是非常大和非常好的东西。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Then for now, aren't you asking home-computer buyers to invest $3000 in what is essentially an act of faith?
units.translation_cn: 那么现在，你是不是要求家用电脑购买者投入3000美元，这本质上是一种信仰行为？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: In the future, it won't be an act of faith. The hard part of what we're up against now is that people ask you about specifics and you can't tell them. A hundred years ago, if somebody had asked Alexander Graham Bell, "What are you going to be able to do with a telephone?" he wouldn't have been able to tell him the ways the telephone would affect the world. He didn't know that people would use the telephone to call up and find out what movies were playing that night or to order some groceries or call a relative on the other side of the globe. But remember that first the public telegraph was inaugurated, in 1844. It was an amazing breakthrough in communications. You could actually send messages from New York to San Francisco in an afternoon. People talked about putting a telegraph on every desk in America to improve productivity. But it wouldn't have worked. It required that people learn this whole sequence of strange incantations, Morse code, dots and dashes, to use the telegraph. It took about 40 hours to learn. The majority of people would never learn how to use it. So, fortunately, in the 1870s, Bell filed the patents for the telephone. It performed basically the same function as the telegraph, but people already knew how to use it. Also, the neatest thing about it was that besides allowing you to communicate with just words, it allowed you to sing.
units.translation_cn: 在未来，这不会是一种信仰行为。我们现在面临的困难是人们问你具体细节，而你无法告诉他们。一百年前，如果有人问亚历山大·格雷厄姆·贝尔，“你用电话能做什么？”他将无法告诉他电话将如何影响世界。他不知道人们会用电话打电话查询当晚播放的电影，或者订购一些杂货，或者给地球另一端的亲戚打电话。但请记住，公共电报于1844年首次启用。这是通信领域的一个惊人突破。你实际上可以在一个下午从纽约向旧金山发送消息。人们谈论在美国的每个办公桌上都安装电报以提高生产力。但它不会奏效。它要求人们学习这一整套奇怪的咒语，莫尔斯电码，点和划，才能使用电报。学习大约需要40个小时。大多数人永远不会学会如何使用它。所以，幸运的是，在1870年代，贝尔为电话申请了专利。它基本上执行了与电报相同的功能，但人们已经知道如何使用它。此外，最棒的是，除了让你只用文字交流之外，它还让你唱歌。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Meaning what?
units.translation_cn: 什么意思？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It allowed you to intone your words with meaning beyond the simple linguistics. And we're in the same situation today. Some people are saying that we ought to put an IBM PC on every desk in America to improve productivity. It won't work. The special incantations you have to learn this time are "slash q-zs" and things like that. The manual for WordStar, the most popular word-processing program, is 400 pages thick. To write a novel, you have to read a novel—one that reads like a mystery to most people. They're not going to learn slash q-z any more than they're going to learn Morse code. That is what Macintosh is all about. It's the first "telephone" of our industry. And, besides that, the neatest thing about it, to me, is that the Macintosh lets you sing the way the telephone did. You don't simply communicate words, you have special print styles and the ability to draw and add pictures to express yourself.
units.translation_cn: 它让你用超越简单语言学的意义来表达你的话语。我们今天也处于同样的情况。有些人说我们应该在美国的每个办公桌上都放一台IBM PC来提高生产力。这行不通。这次你必须学习的特殊咒语是“斜杠q-zs”之类的东西。最流行的文字处理程序WordStar的手册有400页厚。要写一部小说，你必须读一部小说——一部对大多数人来说读起来像悬疑小说的小说。他们不会学习斜杠q-z，就像他们不会学习莫尔斯电码一样。这就是Macintosh的全部意义。它是我们行业的第一部“电话”。而且，除此之外，对我来说，最棒的是Macintosh让你像电话一样唱歌。你不仅仅是交流文字，你还有特殊的打印样式以及绘画和添加图片来表达自己的能力。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Is that really significant or is it simply a novelty? The Macintosh has been called "the world's most expensive Etch A Sketch" by at least one critic.
units.translation_cn: 这真的有意义吗，还是仅仅是一种新奇事物？Macintosh至少被一位评论家称为“世界上最昂贵的Etch A Sketch”。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It's as significant as the difference between the telephone and the telegraph. Imagine what you could have done if you had this sophisticated an Etch A Sketch when you were growing up. But that's only a small part of it. Not only can it help you increase your productivity and your creativity enormously, but it also allows us to communicate more efficiently by using pictures and graphs as well as words and numbers.
units.translation_cn: 它的重要性就像电话和电报之间的区别一样。想象一下，如果你小时候有这样一台复杂版的“蚀刻画板”，你能做些什么。但那还只是很小的一部分。它不仅能极大地提高你的生产力和创造力，还能让我们通过图片和图表，以及文字和数字，更有效地沟通。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Most computers use key strokes to enter instructions, but Macintosh replaces many of them with something called a mouse—a little box that is rolled around on your desk and guides a pointer on your computer screen. It's a big change for people used to keyboards. Why the mouse?
units.translation_cn: 大多数计算机都用按键来输入指令，但 Macintosh 用一种叫鼠标的东西替代了其中许多按键——那是一个可以在桌面上滚动的小盒子，用来引导电脑屏幕上的指针。对于习惯键盘的人来说，这是一个很大的变化。为什么要用鼠标？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: If I want to tell you there is a spot on your shirt, I'm not going to do it linguistically: "There's a spot on your shirt 14 centimeters down from the collar and three centimeters to the left of your button." If you have a spot—"There!" [He points]—I'll point to it. Pointing is a metaphor we all know. We've done a lot of studies and tests on that, and it's much faster to do all kinds of functions, such as cutting and pasting, with a mouse, so it's not only easier to use but more efficient.
units.translation_cn: 如果我想告诉你，你衬衫上有一个污点，我不会用语言来描述：“你的衬衫上，从领口往下 14 厘米的地方有一个点……”如果你身上有一个污点——“在那里！”[他指了一下]——我会直接指给你看。指向，是我们所有人都熟悉的一种隐喻。我们围绕这一点做了很多研究和测试，用鼠标来完成各种功能，比如剪切和粘贴，要快得多。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How long did it take to develop Macintosh?
units.translation_cn: 开发 Macintosh 花了多长时间？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It was more than two years on the computer itself. We had been working on the technology behind it for years before that. I don't think I've ever worked so hard on something, but working on Macintosh was the neatest experience of my life. Almost everyone who worked on it will say that. None of us wanted to release it at the end. It was as though we knew that once it was out of our hands, it wouldn't be ours anymore. When we finally presented it at the shareholders' meeting, everyone in the auditorium stood up and gave it a five-minute ovation. What was incredible to me was that I could see the Mac team in the first few rows. It was as though none of us could believe that we'd actually finished it. Everyone started crying.
units.translation_cn: 单是电脑本身就花了两年多。在那之前，我们已经为它背后的技术工作了好几年。我觉得自己从来没有为任何东西这样拼命工作过，但做 Macintosh 是我一生中最美妙的经历。几乎每一个参与过它的人都会这么说。到最后，我们没有一个人想把它发布出去。那感觉就像我们知道，一旦它离开我们手里，它就不再属于我们了。当我们最终在股东大会上展示它时，礼堂里的每个人都站了起来，给了它长达五分钟的掌声。对我来说不可思议的是，我能看到 Mac 团队坐在最前面的几排。那感觉就像我们所有人都不敢相信自己真的把它完成了。所有人都哭了起来。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: We were warned about you: Before this Interview began, someone said we were "about to be snowed by the best."
units.translation_cn: 我们事先听人提醒过你：这次采访开始前，有人说我们“马上就要被最会说服人的人给糊弄住了”。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: [Smiling] We're just enthusiastic about what we do.
units.translation_cn: [微笑] 我们只是对自己所做的事情充满热情。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: But considering that enthusiasm, the multimillion-dollar ad campaigns and your own ability to get press coverage, how does the consumer know what's behind the hype?
units.translation_cn: 但考虑到这种热情、数百万美元的广告活动，以及你本人获得媒体报道的能力，消费者怎么知道那些宣传背后到底是什么？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Ad campaigns are necessary for competition; IBM's ads are everywhere. But good PR educates people; that's all it is. You can't con people in this business. The products speak for themselves.
units.translation_cn: 广告活动是竞争所必需的；IBM 的广告到处都是。但好的公关是在教育人们；它就是这么回事。你不可能在这个行业里欺骗人。产品会自己说话。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Aside from some of the recurrent criticisms—that the mouse is inefficient, that the Macintosh screen is only black and white—the most serious charge is that Apple overprices its products. Do you care to answer any or all?
units.translation_cn: 除了那些反复出现的批评——比如鼠标效率低，Macintosh 的屏幕只有黑白——最严重的指控是苹果产品定价过高。你愿意回答其中任何一项，或者全部回答吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: We've done studies that prove that the mouse is faster than traditional ways of moving through data or applications. Someday we may be able to build a color screen for a reasonable price. As to overpricing, the start-up of a new product makes it more expensive than it will be later. The more we can produce, the lower the price will get——
units.translation_cn: 我们做过研究，证明鼠标比传统的数据或应用程序浏览方式更快。也许有一天，我们能够以合理的价格做出彩色屏幕。至于定价过高的问题，新产品刚启动时，成本确实会比以后更高。我们生产得越多，价格就会越低——

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: That's what critics charge you with: hooking the enthusiasts with premium prices, then turning around and lowering your prices to catch the rest of the market.
units.translation_cn: 这正是批评者指责你们的地方：先用高价吸引发烧友，然后再转身降价，去争取市场上的其他人。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气和内容更像 Playboy 追问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: That's simply untrue. As soon as we can lower prices, we do. It's true that our computers are less expensive today than they were a few years ago, or even last year. But that's also true of the IBM PC. Our goal is to get computers out to tens of millions of people, and the cheaper we can make them, the easier it's going to be to do that. I'd love it if Macintosh cost $1000.
units.translation_cn: 这完全不是真的。只要我们能够降价，我们就会降价。确实，我们今天的电脑比几年前便宜，甚至比去年也便宜。但 IBM PC 也是这样。我们的目标是让电脑进入数千万人的生活；我们越能把它做便宜，这件事就越容易实现。如果 Macintosh 只卖 1000 美元，我会非常高兴。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How about people who bought Lisa and Apple III, the two computers you released prior to Macintosh? You've left them with incompatible, out-of-date products.
units.translation_cn: 那么那些买了 Lisa 和 Apple III 的人怎么办？这两款电脑都是你们在 Macintosh 之前推出的。你们让他们手里拿着不兼容、已经过时的产品。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: If you want to try that one, add the people who bought the IBM PCs or the PCjrs to that list, too. As far as Lisa is concerned, since some of its technology was used in the Macintosh, it can now run Macintosh software and is being seen as a big brother to Macintosh; though it was unsuccessful at first, our sales of Lisa are going through the roof. We're also still selling more than 2000 Apple IIIs a month—more than half to repeat buyers. The over-all point is that new technology will not necessarily replace old technology, but it will date it. By definition. Eventually, it will replace it. But it's like people who had black-and-white TVs when color came out. They eventually decided whether or not the new technology was worth the investment.
units.translation_cn: 如果你要这么说，那也把买了 IBM PC 或 PCjr 的人加到这个名单里吧。就 Lisa 而言，由于它的一些技术被用在 Macintosh 上，它现在可以运行 Macintosh 软件，也被看作 Macintosh 的大哥；虽然它一开始并不成功，但我们现在 Lisa 的销量正在猛增。我们每个月也仍然卖出 2000 多台 Apple III，其中超过一半卖给了回头客。总体来说，新技术未必会立刻取代旧技术，但它会让旧技术显得过时。这是定义上就如此的事。最终，它会取代旧技术。但这就像彩色电视出现时，那些拥有黑白电视的人一样。他们最终会自己判断，新技术是否值得这笔投入。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: At the rate things are changing, won't Mac itself be out of date within a few years?
units.translation_cn: 照这样的变化速度，Mac 本身不会在几年内也变得过时吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Before Macintosh, there were two standards: Apple II and IBM PC. Those two standards are like rivers carved in the rock bed of a canyon. It's taken years to carve them—seven years to carve the Apple II and four years to carve the IBM. What we have done with Macintosh is that in less than a year, through the momentum of the revolutionary aspects of the product and through every ounce of marketing that we have as a company, we have been able to blast a third channel through that rock and make a third river, a third standard. In my opinion, there are only two companies that can do that today, Apple and IBM. Maybe that's too bad, but to do it right now is just a monumental effort, and I don't think that Apple or IBM will do that in the next three or four years. Toward the end of the Eighties, we may be seeing some new things.
units.translation_cn: 在 Macintosh 之前，有两个标准：Apple II 和 IBM PC。这两个标准就像峡谷岩床中被河水冲刷出来的河道。它们是多年形成的——Apple II 花了七年，IBM 花了四年。而我们用 Macintosh 做到的是，在不到一年的时间里，凭借这款产品革命性特征所带来的动量，以及我们作为一家公司所能调动的每一分营销力量，我们在那块岩石中炸开了第三条通道，形成了第三条河流，也就是第三个标准。在我看来，今天只有两家公司能做到这一点：苹果和 IBM。也许这有点遗憾，但眼下要做到这件事，就是一项巨大的工程。我不认为苹果或 IBM 在未来三四年里还会再做一次。到了八十年代末，我们也许会看到一些新东西。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: And in the meantime?
units.translation_cn: 那在此之前呢？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: The developments will be in making the products more and more portable, networking them, getting out laser printers, getting out shared data bases, getting out more communications ability, maybe the merging of the telephone and the personal computer.
units.translation_cn: 发展方向会是让产品越来越便携，把它们联网，推出激光打印机，推出共享数据库，增强通信能力，也许还会让电话和个人电脑融合在一起。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You have a lot riding on this one. Some people have said that Macintosh will make or break Apple. After Lisa and Apple III, Apple stock plummeted and the industry speculated that Apple might not survive.
units.translation_cn: 你们在这款产品上押了很多。有些人说，Macintosh 会决定苹果的成败。在 Lisa 和 Apple III 之后，苹果股价暴跌，整个行业都在猜测苹果可能撑不下去。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Yeah, we felt the weight of the world on our shoulders. We knew that we had to pull the rabbit out of the hat with Macintosh, or else we'd never realize the dreams we had for either the products or the company.
units.translation_cn: 是的，我们觉得整个世界的重量都压在肩上。我们知道，我们必须靠 Macintosh 从帽子里变出兔子，否则我们对产品和公司所怀抱的梦想就永远无法实现。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How serious was it? Was Apple near bankruptcy?
units.translation_cn: 情况有多严重？苹果当时接近破产了吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: No, no, no. In fact, 1983, when all these predictions were being made, was a phenomenally successful year for Apple. We virtually doubled in size in 1983. We went from $583,000,000 in 1982 to something like $980,000,000 in sales. It was almost all Apple II-related. It just didn't live up to our expectations. If Macintosh weren't a success, we probably would have stayed at something like a billion dollars a year, selling Apple IIs and versions of it.
units.translation_cn: 不，不，不。事实上，1983 年，也就是这些预测满天飞的时候，对苹果来说是极其成功的一年。1983 年，我们的规模几乎翻了一番。我们的销售额从 1982 年的 5.83 亿美元增长到大约 9.8 亿美元。几乎全部都和 Apple II 有关，只是它没有达到我们的预期。如果 Macintosh 没有成功，我们大概也会维持在每年十亿美元左右的规模，继续销售 Apple II 以及它的各种版本。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Then what was behind the talk last year that Apple had had it?
units.translation_cn: 那去年那些说苹果已经完了的说法，背后到底是什么原因？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: IBM was coming on very, very strong, and the momentum was switching to IBM. The software developers were moving to IBM. The dealers were talking more and more of IBM. It became clear to all of us who worked on Macintosh that it was just gonna blow the socks off the industry, that it was going to redefine the industry. And that's exactly what it had to do. If Macintosh hadn't been successful, then I should have just thrown in the towel, because my vision of the whole industry would have been totally wrong.
units.translation_cn: IBM 的势头变得非常、非常强，整个动量正在转向 IBM。软件开发者正在转向 IBM。经销商也越来越多地谈论 IBM。对我们所有参与 Macintosh 的人来说，事情已经很清楚：它将震动整个行业，将重新定义这个行业。而它也确实必须做到这一点。如果 Macintosh 没有成功，那我就该直接认输了，因为我对整个行业的愿景就会是完全错误的。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Apple III was supposed to have been your souped-up Apple II, but it has been a failure since it was launched, four years ago. You recalled the first 14,000, and even the revised Apple III never took off. How much was lost on Apple III?
units.translation_cn: Apple III 原本应该是你们加强版的 Apple II，但自从四年前推出以来，它一直是个失败产品。你们召回了最初的 14000 台，即使是修订后的 Apple III 也始终没有起飞。Apple III 上损失了多少钱？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Infinite, incalculable amounts. I think if the III had been more successful, IBM would have had a much harder time entering the market place. But that's life. I think we emerged from that experience much stronger.
units.translation_cn: 无限的、无法计算的数额。我认为，如果 Apple III 更成功一些，IBM 进入这个市场会困难得多。但生活就是这样。我认为我们从那次经历中走出来后，变得强大得多。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Yet when Lisa came out, it, too, was a relative failure in the market place. What went wrong?
units.translation_cn: 可是 Lisa 推出时，在市场上也算是相对失败。哪里出了问题？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: First of all, it was too expensive—about ten grand. We had gotten Fortune 500-itis, trying to sell to those huge corporations, when our roots were selling to people. There were other problems: late shipping; the software didn't come together in the end as well as we hoped and we lost a lot of momentum. And IBM's coming on very strong, coupled with our being about six months late, coupled with the price's being too high, plus another strategic mistake we made—deciding to sell Lisa only through about 150 dealers, which was absolutely foolish on our part—meant it was a very costly mistake. We decided to hire people we thought were marketing and management experts. Not a bad idea, but unfortunately, this was such a new business that the things the so-called professionals knew were almost detriments to their success in this new way of looking at business.
units.translation_cn: 首先，它太贵了——大约一万美元。我们染上了“《财富》500 强病”，试图把它卖给那些巨型公司，而我们的根本是把电脑卖给人。还有其他问题：发货延迟；软件最终没有像我们希望的那样整合起来，我们失去了很多势头。再加上 IBM 来势很猛，我们又晚了大约六个月，价格也太高，还有另一个战略错误——决定只通过大约 150 家经销商销售 Lisa，这绝对是我们愚蠢之举——所以这是一次代价高昂的错误。我们决定聘请一些我们认为是营销和管理专家的人。这不是一个坏主意，但不幸的是，这个行业太新了，所谓专业人士所知道的东西，在这种看待商业的新方式里，几乎反而成了他们成功的障碍。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Was that a reflection of insecurity on your part—"This thing has gotten big and now we're playing hardball; I better bring in some real pros"?
units.translation_cn: 这是否反映了你们某种不安全感——“这件事已经变大了，现在我们是在打硬仗；我最好请一些真正的专业人士进来”？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Remember, we were 23, 24 and 25 years old. We had never done any of this before, so it seemed like a good thing to do.
units.translation_cn: 别忘了，我们当时只有二十三、二十四、二十五岁。我们以前从没做过这些事，所以那看起来像是一件值得做的事。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Were most of those decisions, good and bad, yours?
units.translation_cn: 那些决定，无论好坏，大多数都是你做的吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: We tried never to have one person make all the decisions. There were three people running the company at that time: Mike Scott, Mike Markkula and myself. Now it's John Sculley [Apple's president] and myself. In the early days, if there was a disagreement, I would generally defer my judgment to some of the other people who had more experience than I had. In many cases, they were right. In some important cases, if we had gone my way, we would have done better.
units.translation_cn: 我们一直尽量避免让一个人做所有决定。当时经营公司的是三个人：迈克·斯科特、迈克·马库拉和我。现在是约翰·斯卡利[苹果总裁]和我。在早期，如果出现分歧，我通常会把自己的判断让位给那些比我更有经验的人。很多时候，他们是对的。但在一些重要事情上，如果当时按我的想法做，我们会做得更好。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You wanted to run the Lisa division. Markkula and Scott, who were, in effect, your bosses, even though you had a hand in hiring them, didn't feel you were capable, right?
units.translation_cn: 你曾经想管理 Lisa 部门。马库拉和斯科特实际上是你的上司，虽然你也参与过聘请他们，但他们觉得你没有这个能力，对吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: After setting up the framework for the concepts and finding the key people and sort of setting the technical directions, Scotty decided I didn't have the experience to run the thing. It hurt a lot. There's no getting around it.
units.translation_cn: 在搭好概念框架、找到关键人员，并大致确定技术方向之后，斯科蒂觉得我没有经验来负责这个项目。这让我很受伤。这一点无法回避。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Did you feel you were losing Apple?
units.translation_cn: 你当时觉得自己正在失去苹果吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: There was a bit of that, I guess, but the thing that was harder for me was that they hired a lot of people in the Lisa group who didn't share the vision we originally had. There was a big conflict in the Lisa group between the people who wanted, in essence, to build something like Macintosh and the people hired from Hewlett-Packard and other companies who brought with them a perspective of larger machines, corporate sales. I just decided that I was going to go off and do that myself with a small group, sort of go back to the garage, to design the Macintosh. They didn't take us very seriously. I think Scotty was just sort of humoring me.
units.translation_cn: 我想多少有一点。但对我来说更难受的是，他们在 Lisa 团队里招了很多人，而这些人并不认同我们最初的愿景。Lisa 团队内部有很大的冲突：一边是那些本质上想做出类似 Macintosh 的东西的人，另一边是从惠普和其他公司招来的人，他们带来的是大型机器、企业销售那一套视角。于是我决定自己带一个小团队去做那件事，有点像回到车库，去设计 Macintosh。他们并没有太认真地看待我们。我觉得斯科蒂只是有点在迁就我。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: But this was the company that you founded. Weren't you resentful?
units.translation_cn: 但这是你创办的公司。你没有怨恨吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: You can never resent your kid.
units.translation_cn: 你永远不会怨恨自己的孩子。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Even when your kid tells you to fuck off?
units.translation_cn: 即使你的孩子叫你滚蛋？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I wouldn't feel resentment. I'd feel great sorrow about it and I'd be frustrated, which I was. But I got the best people who were at Apple, because I thought that if we didn't do that, we'd be in real trouble. Of course, it was those people who came up with Macintosh. [Shrugs] Look at Mac.
units.translation_cn: 我不会感到怨恨。我会对此感到非常悲伤，也会感到挫败——而我确实感到过挫败。但我把苹果最好的人都找来了，因为我认为如果我们不这样做，我们就会真的陷入麻烦。当然，也正是这些人做出了 Macintosh。[耸肩] 看看 Mac 吧。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: That verdict is far from in. In fact, you ushered in the Mac with a lot of the same fanfare that preceded the Lisa, and the Lisa failed initially.
units.translation_cn: 这个结论还远远没有定论。事实上，你们推出 Mac 时用了很多和当初推出 Lisa 前一样的造势方式，而 Lisa 一开始失败了。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气和内容更像 Playboy 追问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It's true: We expressed very high hopes for Lisa and we were wrong. The hardest thing for us was that we knew Macintosh was coming, and Macintosh seemed to overcome every possible objection to Lisa. As a company, we would be getting back to our roots—selling computers to people, not corporations. We went off and built the most insanely great computer in the world.
units.translation_cn: 这是真的：我们曾经对 Lisa 表达了很高的期待，而我们错了。对我们来说，最难的一点是，我们知道 Macintosh 就要来了，而 Macintosh 似乎克服了人们对 Lisa 可能提出的每一种反对意见。作为一家公司，我们会回到自己的根上——把电脑卖给人，而不是卖给公司。我们离开去做出了世界上最疯狂伟大的电脑。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Does it take insane people to make insanely great things?
units.translation_cn: 要做出疯狂伟大的东西，需要疯狂的人吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Actually, making an insanely great product has a lot to do with the process of making the product, how you learn things and adopt new ideas and throw out old ideas. But, yeah, the people who made Mac are sort of on the edge.
units.translation_cn: 实际上，做出一件疯狂伟大的产品，很大程度上和制作产品的过程有关，和你如何学习，如何采纳新想法，如何抛弃旧想法有关。不过，是的，做出 Mac 的那群人确实有点处在边缘状态。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What's the difference between the people who have insanely great ideas and the people who pull off those insanely great ideas?
units.translation_cn: 那些拥有疯狂伟大想法的人，和那些真正把疯狂伟大想法做出来的人，区别在哪里？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Let me compare it with IBM. How come the Mac group produced Mac and the people at IBM produced the PCjr? We think the Mac will sell zillions, but we didn't build Mac for anybody else. We built it for ourselves. We were the group of people who were going to judge whether it was great or not. We weren't going to go out and do market research. We just wanted to build the best thing we could build. When you're a carpenter making a beautiful chest of drawers, you're not going to use a piece of plywood on the back, even though it faces the wall and nobody will ever see it. You'll know it's there, so you're going to use a beautiful piece of wood on the back. For you to sleep well at night, the aesthetic, the quality, has to be carried all the way through.
units.translation_cn: 让我拿 IBM 作个比较。为什么 Mac 团队做出了 Mac，而 IBM 的人做出了 PCjr？我们认为 Mac 会卖出很多很多台，但我们并不是为别人做 Mac。我们是为自己做的。我们就是那群要判断它到底伟不伟大的人。我们不会出去做市场调研。我们只是想做出我们能做出的最好的东西。当你是一个木匠，要做一个漂亮的抽屉柜时，你不会因为背面朝墙、永远没人看见，就在背面用一块胶合板。你知道它在那里，所以你会在背面也用一块漂亮的木头。为了让你晚上睡得安稳，美感和质量必须贯穿到底。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Are you saying that the people who made the PCjr don't have that kind of pride in the product?
units.translation_cn: 你的意思是，做 PCjr 的那些人没有这种对产品的自豪感？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: If they did, they wouldn't have turned out the PCjr. It seems clear to me that they were designing that on the basis of market research for a specific market segment, for a specific demographic type of customer, and they hoped that if they built this, lots of people would buy them and they'd make lots of money. Those are different motivations. The people in the Mac group wanted to build the greatest computer that has ever been seen.
units.translation_cn: 如果他们有，就不会做出 PCjr。对我来说很清楚，他们是基于某个特定市场细分、某种特定人口类型顾客的市场调研来设计它的。他们希望如果做出这样一台机器，会有很多人购买，他们就能赚很多钱。那是不同的动机。Mac 团队的人想做出有史以来最伟大的电脑。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Why is the computer field dominated by people so young? The average age of Apple employees is 29.
units.translation_cn: 为什么计算机领域会被这么年轻的人主导？苹果员工的平均年龄是 29 岁。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It's often the same with any new, revolutionary thing. People get stuck as they get older. Our minds are sort of electrochemical computers. Your thoughts construct patterns like scaffolding in your mind. You are really etching chemical patterns. In most cases, people get stuck in those patterns, just like grooves in a record, and they never get out of them. It's a rare person who etches grooves that are other than a specific way of looking at things, a specific way of questioning things. It's rare that you see an artist in his 30s or 40s able to really contribute something amazing. Of course, there are some people who are innately curious, forever little kids in their awe of life, but they're rare.
units.translation_cn: 任何新的、革命性的东西，往往都是这样。人年纪大了以后就会被困住。我们的头脑有点像电化学计算机。你的思想会在脑海中构建出像脚手架一样的模式。你实际上是在蚀刻化学模式。大多数情况下，人会被困在这些模式里，就像唱片上的沟槽一样，再也走不出来。很少有人会刻出不同于某一种固定看法、某一种固定提问方式的沟槽。你很少看到三四十岁的艺术家还能真正贡献出令人惊叹的东西。当然，有些人天生好奇，永远像小孩子一样，对生命充满敬畏，但这样的人很少。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: A lot of guys in their 40s are going to be real pleased with you. Let's move on to the other thing that people talk about when they mention Apple—the company, not the computer. You feel a similar sense of mission about the way things are run at Apple, don't you?
units.translation_cn: 很多四十多岁的家伙听到你这话会很高兴。我们转到另一个话题：人们提到苹果时谈论的另一件事——公司，而不是电脑。你对苹果的运营方式也有一种类似的使命感，对吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I do feel there is another way we have an effect on society besides our computers. I think Apple has a chance to be the model of a Fortune 500 company in the late Eighties and early Nineties. Ten to 15 years ago, if you asked people to make a list of the five most exciting companies in America, Polaroid and Xerox would have been on everyone's list. Where are they now? They would be on no one's list today. What happened? Companies, as they grow to become multibillion-dollar entities, somehow lose their vision. They insert lots of layers of middle management between the people running the company and the people doing the work. They no longer have an inherent feel or a passion about the products. The creative people, who are the ones who care passionately, have to persuade five layers of management to do what they know is the right thing to do.What happens in most companies is that you don't keep great people under working environments where individual accomplishment is discouraged rather than encouraged. The great people leave and you end up with mediocrity. I know, because that's how Apple was built. Apple is an Ellis Island company. Apple is built on refugees from other companies. These are the extremely bright individual contributors who were troublemakers at other companies. You know, Dr. Edwin Land was a troublemaker. He dropped out of Harvard and founded Polaroid. Not only was he one of the great inventors of our time but, more important, he saw the intersection of art and science and business and built an organization to reflect that. Polaroid did that for some years, but eventually Dr. Land, one of those brilliant troublemakers, was asked to leave his own company—which is one of the dumbest things I've ever heard of. So Land, at 75, went off to spend the remainder of his life doing pure science, trying to crack the code of color vision. The man is a national treasure. I don't understand why people like that can't be held up as models: This is the most incredible thing to be—not an astronaut, not a football player—but this. Anyway, one of our biggest challenges, and the one I think John Sculley and I should be judged on in five to ten years, is making Apple an incredibly great ten- or 20-billion-dollar company. Will it still have the spirit it does today? We're charting new territory. There are no models that we can look to for our high growth, for some of the new management concepts we have. So we're having to find our own way.
units.translation_cn: 我确实觉得，除了我们的电脑之外，我们还有另一种影响社会的方式。我认为，苹果有机会成为八十年代末、九十年代初《财富》500 强公司的典范。十到十五年前，如果你让人们列出美国最令人兴奋的五家公司，宝丽来和施乐会出现在每个人的名单上。可它们现在在哪里？今天没有人的名单上会有它们。发生了什么？公司在成长为数十亿美元规模的实体时，不知怎么就失去了愿景。它们在经营公司的人和真正做事的人之间插入了许多层中层管理。它们不再对产品有内在的感觉或热情。真正关心产品、充满热情的创造性人才，必须说服五层管理者，才能去做他们明知道正确的事情。大多数公司发生的问题是：你无法在一种不鼓励个人成就、反而压抑个人成就的工作环境中留住优秀的人。优秀的人离开了，剩下的就是平庸。我知道这一点，因为苹果就是这样建立起来的。苹果是一家“埃利斯岛式”的公司，是由来自其他公司的“难民”建立起来的。这些人都是极其聪明的个人贡献者，在其他公司里他们是麻烦制造者。埃德温·兰德博士就是一个麻烦制造者。他从哈佛退学，创办了宝丽来。他不仅是我们这个时代最伟大的发明家之一；更重要的是，他看到了艺术、科学和商业的交汇点，并建立了一个能够体现这种交汇的组织。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: If Apple is really that kind of company, then why the projected twenty-fold growth? Why not stay relatively small?
units.translation_cn: 宝丽来曾经这样运转了好几年，但最终，兰德博士——这样一位杰出的麻烦制造者——被要求离开他自己的公司。这是我听过的最愚蠢的事情之一。于是兰德在 75 岁时离开，去用余生做纯科学研究，试图破解色彩视觉的密码。这个人是国家级的珍宝。我不明白，为什么不能把这样的人树立为榜样：最了不起的事情不是成为宇航员，不是成为橄榄球运动员，而是成为这样的人。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: The way it's going to work out is that in our business, in order to continue to be one of the major contributors, we're going to have to be a ten-billion-dollar company. That growth is required for us to keep up with the competition. Our concern is how we become that, rather than the dollar goal, which is meaningless to us. At Apple, people are putting in 18-hour days. We attract a different type of person—a person who doesn't want to wait five or ten years to have someone take a giant risk on him or her. Someone who really wants to get in a little over his head and make a little dent in the universe. We are aware that we are doing something significant. We're here at the beginning of it and we're able to shape how it goes. Everyone here has the sense that right now is one of those moments when we are influencing the future. Most of the time, we're taking things. Neither you nor I made the clothes we wear; we don't make the food or grow the foods we eat; we use a language that was developed by other people; we use another society's mathematics. Very rarely do we get a chance to put something back into that pool. I think we have that opportunity now. And no, we don't know where it will lead. We just know there's something much bigger than any of us here.
units.translation_cn: 无论如何，我们最大的挑战之一，也是我认为五到十年后人们应该用来评价约翰·斯卡利和我的事情，就是把苹果变成一家极其伟大的、价值 100 亿或 200 亿美元的公司。到那时，它还会保有今天这种精神吗？我们正在开辟新的领域。对于我们的高速成长，对于我们拥有的一些新的管理概念，我们没有可以参照的模式。所以我们必须找到自己的路。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You've said that the business market is crucial for you to conquer with Macintosh. Can you beat IBM at work?
units.translation_cn: 如果苹果真是这样一种公司，那为什么还要规划二十倍的增长？为什么不保持相对小的规模？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Yes. The business market has several sectors. Rather than just thinking of the Fortune 500, which is where IBM is strongest, I like to think of the Fortune 5,000,000 or 14,000,000. There are 14,000,000 small businesses in this country. I think that the vast group of people who need to be computerized includes that large number of medium and small businesses. We're going to try to be able to bring some meaningful solutions to them in 1985.
units.translation_cn: 事情会这样发展：在我们的行业里，如果要继续成为主要贡献者之一，我们就必须成为一家百亿美元级的公司。为了跟上竞争，这种增长是必需的。我们关心的是如何成为那样的公司，而不是那个美元目标本身；那个数字对我们来说没有意义。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: How?
units.translation_cn: 在苹果，人们每天工作 18 个小时。我们吸引的是一种不同类型的人——他们不想等五年或十年，才有人愿意在他们身上冒一次巨大的风险。他们真的想让自己稍微超出能力范围一点，想在宇宙中留下一个小小的凹痕。我们知道自己正在做一件重要的事情。我们处在它的开端，并且能够塑造它的发展方式。这里的每个人都有一种感觉：此刻正是那种我们正在影响未来的时刻。大多数时候，我们只是在取用东西。你和我身上穿的衣服都不是我们做的；我们不生产食物，也不种植我们吃的东西；我们使用的是别人发展出来的语言；我们使用的是另一个社会发展出的数学。我们很少有机会把某些东西重新投入这个共同的池子里。我认为我们现在就有这样的机会。而且，不，我们不知道它会通向哪里。我们只知道，这里有某种远远大于我们任何一个人的东西。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Our approach is to think of them not as businesses but as collections of people. We want to qualitatively change the way people work. We don't just want to help them do word processing faster or add numbers faster. We want to change the way they can communicate with one another. We're seeing five-page memos get compressed to one-page memos because we can use a picture to express the key concept. We're seeing less paper flying around and more quality of communication. And it's more fun. There's always been this myth that really neat, fun people at home all of a sudden have to become very dull and boring when they come to work. It's simply not true. If we can inject that liberal-arts spirit into the very serious realm of business, I think it will be a worthwhile contribution. We can't even conceive of how far it will go.
units.translation_cn: 你曾经说过，对你们来说，用 Macintosh 征服商业市场至关重要。你们能在工作场景中击败 IBM 吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: But in the business market, you're fighting the IBM name as much as anything. People associate IBM with stability and efficiency. The new entry in the computer field, A.T.&T., has that one up on you, too. Apple is a relatively young and untested company, particularly in the eyes of corporations that might be customers.
units.translation_cn: 能。商业市场有几个部分。与其只想着《财富》500 强——那是 IBM 最强的地方——我更愿意去想《财富》500 万强，甚至 1400 万强。这个国家有 1400 万家小企业。我认为，需要被计算机化的庞大人群，包括数量巨大的中小企业。我们会努力在 1985 年给他们带去一些有意义的解决方案。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: It's Macintosh's job to really penetrate the business market place. IBM focuses on the top down, the mainframe centric approach to selling in businesses. If we are going to be successful, we've got to approach this from a grass-roots point of view. To use networking as an example, rather than focusing on wiring up whole companies, as IBM is doing, we're going to focus on the phenomenon of the small work group.
units.translation_cn: 怎么做？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: One of the experts in the field says that for this industry to really flourish, and for it to benefit the consumer, one standard has to prevail.
units.translation_cn: 我们的做法是，不把它们看作企业，而是看作一群人的集合。我们想从质上改变人们的工作方式。我们不只是想帮助他们更快地做文字处理，或者更快地加数字。我们想改变他们彼此沟通的方式。我们看到五页的备忘录被压缩成一页，因为我们可以用一张图来表达关键概念。我们看到纸张飞来飞去的情况减少了，沟通质量提高了。而且这更有趣。一直以来都有这样一种神话：那些在家里非常聪明、有趣的人，一到工作中就突然不得不变得非常沉闷无聊。这根本不是真的。如果我们能把这种人文精神注入非常严肃的商业领域，我认为那会是一项值得做的贡献。我们甚至无法想象它会走多远。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: That's simply untrue. Insisting that we need one standard now is like saying that they needed one standard for automobiles in 1920. There would have been no innovations such as the automatic transmission, power steering and independent suspension if they believed that. The last thing we want to do is freeze technology. With computers, Macintosh is revolutionary. There is no question that Macintosh's technology is superior to IBM's. There is a clear need for an alternative to IBM.
units.translation_cn: 但在商业市场上，你们要对抗的，很大程度上也是 IBM 这个名字。人们把 IBM 和稳定、高效联系在一起。计算机领域的新进入者 AT&T 在这一点上也比你们有优势。苹果是一家相对年轻、还没有经过充分检验的公司，尤其是在那些可能成为客户的大公司眼中。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气和内容更像 Playboy 追问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Was any of your decision not to become compatible with IBM based on the fact that you didn't want to knuckle under to IBM? One critic says that the reason Mac isn't IBM-compatible is mere arrogance—that "Steve Jobs was saying ‘Fuck you' to IBM."
units.translation_cn: 真正打入商业市场，是 Macintosh 的任务。IBM 在企业销售中采取的是自上而下、以大型主机为中心的方式。如果我们要成功，就必须从基层的角度来做这件事。以联网为例，与其像 IBM 那样把重点放在给整个公司布线，我们会把重点放在小型工作组这个现象上。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: It wasn't that we had to express our manhood by being different, no.
units.translation_cn: 这个领域的一位专家说，如果这个行业要真正繁荣，并且让消费者受益，就必须由一个标准占据主导地位。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气和内容更像 Playboy 引述专家观点发问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Then why were you?
units.translation_cn: 这完全不是真的。坚持认为我们现在需要一个标准，就像说 1920 年的汽车行业也需要一个标准一样。如果他们当时相信这一点，就不会有自动变速箱、动力转向和独立悬挂这样的创新。我们最不想做的事情，就是把技术冻结住。就计算机而言，Macintosh 是革命性的。毫无疑问，Macintosh 的技术优于 IBM。市场显然需要一种 IBM 之外的替代选择。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: The main thing is very simply that the technology we developed is superior. It could not be this good if we became compatible with IBM. Of course, it's true that we don't want IBM to dominate this industry. A lot of people thought we were nuts for not being IBM-compatible, for not living under IBM's umbrella. There were two key reasons we chose to bet our company on not doing that: The first was that we thought—and I think as history is unfolding, we're being proved correct—that IBM would fold its umbrella on the companies making compatible computers and absolutely crush them. Second and more important, we did not go IBM-compatible because of the product vision that drives this company. We think that computers are the most remarkable tools that humankind has ever come up with, and we think that people are basically tool users. So if we can just get lots of computers to lots of people, it will make some qualitative difference in the world. What we want to do at Apple is make computers into appliances and get them to tens of millions of people. That's simply what we want to do. And we couldn't do that with the current IBM-generation type of technology. So we had to do something different. That's why we came up with the Macintosh.
units.translation_cn: 你们决定不与 IBM 兼容，其中有没有一部分原因是你们不想向 IBM 低头？有一位批评者说，Mac 不兼容 IBM 的原因只是傲慢——也就是说，“史蒂夫·乔布斯是在对 IBM 说‘去你的’”。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: From 1981 to 1983, your share of the personal-computer sales slipped from 29 percent to 23 percent. IBM's part has grown from three percent to 28 percent in the same time. How do you fight the numbers?
units.translation_cn: 不是因为我们必须通过与众不同来证明自己的男子气概，不是。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: We've never worried about numbers. In the market place, Apple is trying to focus the spotlight on products, because products really make a difference. IBM is trying to focus the spotlight on service, support, security, mainframes and motherhood. Now, Apple's key observation three years ago was that when you're shipping 10,000,000 computers a year, even IBM does not have enough mothers to ship one with every computer. So you've got to build motherhood into the computer. And that's a big part of what Macintosh is all about. All these things show that it really is coming down to just Apple and IBM. If, for some reason, we make some giant mistakes and IBM wins, my personal feeling is that we are going to enter sort of a computer Dark Ages for about 20 years. Once IBM gains control of a market sector, they almost always stop innovation. They prevent innovation from happening.
units.translation_cn: 那你们为什么要这么做？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Why?
units.translation_cn: 最主要的原因非常简单：我们开发出来的技术更优越。如果我们变成与 IBM 兼容，它就不可能做到这么好。当然，我们确实不希望 IBM 主导这个行业。很多人认为我们疯了，因为我们没有做 IBM 兼容，没有活在 IBM 的保护伞之下。我们之所以选择把公司押在不这样做上，有两个关键原因。第一个原因是，我们认为——而且我觉得随着历史展开，事实正在证明我们是对的——IBM 会把它的保护伞收起来，把那些制造兼容电脑的公司彻底压垮。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Look at this example: Frito-Lay is a very interesting company. They call on more than half a million accounts a week. There's a Frito-Lay rack in each store, and the chips are all there, and every store's got the identical rack and the big ones have multiples. For Frito-Lay, the biggest problem is stale product—bad chips, so to speak. For Frito-Lay's service, they've got, like, 10,000 guys who run around and take out the stale product and replace it with good product. They talk to the manager of that department and they make sure everything's fine. Because of that service and support, they now have more than an 80 percent share of every segment of chips that they're in. Nobody else can break into that. As long as they keep doing what they do well, nobody else can get 80 percent of the market share, because they can't get the sales and support staff. They can't get it because they can't afford it. They can't afford it because they don't have 80 percent of the market share. It's catch-22. Nobody will ever be able to break into their franchise. Frito-Lay doesn't have to innovate very much. They just watch all the little chip companies come out with something new, study it for a year, and a year or two years later they come out with their own, service and support it to death, and they've got 80 percent of the market share of the new product a year later. IBM is playing exactly the same game. If you look at the mainframe market place, there's been virtually zero innovation since IBM got dominant control of that market place 15 years ago. They are going to do the same thing in every other sector of the computer market place if they can get away with it. The IBM PC fundamentally brought no new technology to the industry at all. It was just repackaging and slight extension of Apple II technology, and they want it all. They absolutely want it all. This market place is coming down to the two of us, whether we like it or not. I don't particularly like it, but it's coming down to Apple and IBM.
units.translation_cn: 第二个原因，也更重要的是，我们没有走 IBM 兼容路线，是因为驱动这家公司的产品愿景。我们认为，计算机是人类迄今为止发明出的最了不起的工具；我们也认为，人从根本上说就是工具的使用者。所以，只要我们能把大量计算机交到大量人手里，它就会让世界发生某种质的变化。我们在苹果想做的，就是把计算机变成家用电器一样的东西，并把它们带给数千万人。这就是我们想做的事。用当下这一代 IBM 技术，我们做不到这一点。所以我们必须做一些不同的东西。这就是我们为什么做出了 Macintosh。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.translation_cn: 从 1981 年到 1983 年，你们在个人电脑销售中的份额从 29% 下滑到 23%。同期，IBM 的份额从 3% 增长到 28%。你们怎么应对这些数字？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.translation_cn: 我们从来不担心数字。在市场上，苹果试图把聚光灯对准产品，因为产品才真正会带来差异。IBM 则试图把聚光灯对准服务、支持、安全、大型机，以及“母性关怀”。而苹果三年前的关键观察是：当你一年要出货一千万台电脑时，即使是 IBM，也没有足够多的“母亲”可以随每台电脑一起出货。所以你必须把这种“母性关怀”内建到电脑里。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.translation_cn: 这正是 Macintosh 的很大一部分意义所在。所有这些都表明，事情确实正在归结为苹果和 IBM 两家公司。如果由于某种原因，我们犯了巨大的错误而 IBM 赢了，我个人的感觉是，我们会进入某种大约持续 20 年的计算机黑暗时代。一旦 IBM 控制了一个市场领域，它几乎总是会停止创新。它会阻止创新发生。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.translation_cn: 为什么？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.translation_cn: 看看这个例子：Frito-Lay 是一家很有意思的公司。他们每周拜访超过五十万个客户。每家商店里都有一个 Frito-Lay 货架，薯片都摆在那里，每家店的货架都一样，大店里还会有多个。对 Frito-Lay 来说，最大的问题是产品变陈——也就是坏薯片。为了提供服务，他们有大约一万个人四处奔走，把过期变陈的产品拿走，换上好的产品。他们会和那个部门的经理交谈，确保一切正常。正因为有这种服务和支持，他们现在在自己进入的每一个薯片细分市场里都拥有超过 80% 的份额。没有别人能打进去。只要他们继续把自己擅长的事情做好，别人就拿不到 80% 的市场份额，因为别人无法建立销售和支持人员队伍；而他们之所以无法建立，是因为他们负担不起；他们之所以负担不起，是因为他们没有 80% 的市场份额。这就是第二十二条军规。没有人能打破他们的特许领地。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.translation_cn: Frito-Lay 不需要进行太多创新。他们只要看着所有小型薯片公司推出新东西，研究一年，然后一年或两年后推出自己的版本，用服务和支持把它压死，一年后他们就拿到了这个新产品 80% 的市场份额。IBM 正在玩完全相同的游戏。如果你看看大型机市场，自从 IBM 在 15 年前取得主导控制以来，那里几乎没有任何创新。如果它能得逞，它会在计算机市场的每一个其他领域做同样的事情。IBM PC 根本没有给这个行业带来任何新技术。它只是对 Apple II 技术的重新包装和轻微延伸，而他们想要全部。他们绝对想要全部。不管我们喜不喜欢，这个市场正在归结为我们两家。我并不特别喜欢这一点，但它正在变成苹果和 IBM 的局面。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How can you say that about an industry that's changing so fast? Macintosh is the hot new thing right now, but will it still be in two years? Aren't you competing with your own philosophy? Just as you're after IBM, aren't there small computer companies coming after Apple?
units.translation_cn: 对于一个变化如此之快的行业，你怎么能这么说？Macintosh 现在是热门新事物，但两年后还会是吗？你不是也在和自己的哲学竞争吗？就像你们在追赶 IBM 一样，难道没有一些小型计算机公司正在追赶苹果吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: In terms of supplying the computer itself, it's coming down to Apple and IBM. And I don't think there are going to be a lot of third- and fourth-place companies, much less sixth- or seventh-place companies. Most of the new, innovative companies are focusing on the software. I think there will be lots of innovation in the areas of software but not in hardware.
units.translation_cn: 就供应电脑本身而言，局面正在变成苹果和 IBM 之间的竞争。而且我不认为还会有很多第三名、第四名的公司，更不用说第六名、第七名的公司了。大多数新的、创新型公司都在专注于软件。我认为软件领域会有大量创新，但硬件领域不会。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: IBM might say the same thing about hardware, but you're not about to let it get away with that. Why is your point any different?
units.translation_cn: IBM 也许会对硬件说同样的话，但你显然不会让它这么轻易过关。为什么你的观点有什么不同？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I think that the scale of the business has gotten large enough so that it's going to be very difficult for anyone to successfully launch anything new.
units.translation_cn: 我认为这个行业的规模已经变得足够大，以至于任何人想成功推出某种新东西都会非常困难。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: No more billion-dollar companies hatched in garages?
units.translation_cn: 不会再有从车库里孵化出来的十亿美元公司了？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: No, I'm afraid not in computers. And this puts a responsibility on Apple, because if there's going to be innovation in this industry, it'll come from us. It's the only way we can compete with them. If we go fast enough, they can't keep up.
units.translation_cn: 不会了，恐怕在电脑行业不会了。这也给苹果带来了一种责任，因为如果这个行业还会有创新，那它就会来自我们。这是我们与他们竞争的唯一方式。如果我们跑得足够快，他们就跟不上。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: When do you think IBM will finally, as you put it, fold the umbrella on the companies making IBM-compatible computers?
units.translation_cn: 你认为 IBM 什么时候最终会像你说的那样，收起给那些制造 IBM 兼容电脑公司的“保护伞”？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: There may be some imitators left in the $100,000,000-to-$200,000,000 range, but being a $200,000,000 company is going to mean you are struggling for your life, and that's not really a position from which to innovate. Not only do I think IBM will do away with its imitators by providing software they can't provide, I think eventually it will come up with a new standard that won't even be compatible with what it's making now—because it is to limiting.
units.translation_cn: 也许还会剩下一些规模在 1 亿到 2 亿美元之间的模仿者，但成为一家 2 亿美元规模的公司，将意味着你是在为生存而挣扎，而那并不是一个真正能够创新的位置。我不仅认为 IBM 会通过提供那些模仿者无法提供的软件来消灭它们，我还认为 IBM 最终会推出一个新的标准，甚至不会再与它现在制造的东西兼容——因为现在的东西太受限制了。
units.translator_note: 原文 likely 为 too limiting，源文本作 to limiting，按语义译为“太受限制”。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Which is exactly what you've done at Apple. If a person owns software for the Apple II, he can't run it on the Macintosh.
units.translation_cn: 这不正是你们在苹果所做的事吗？如果一个人拥有 Apple II 的软件，他不能在 Macintosh 上运行它。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: That's right. Mac is altogether new. We knew that we could reach the early innovators with current-generation technology—Apple II, IBM PC—because they'd stay up all night learning how to use their computer. But we'd never reach the majority of people. If we were really going to get computers to tens of millions of people, we needed a technology that would make the thing radically easier to use and more powerful at the same time, so we had to make a break. We just had to do it. We wanted to make sure it was great, because it may be the last chance that any of us get to make a clean break. And I'm very happy with the way Macintosh turned out. It will prove a really solid foundation for the next ten years.
units.translation_cn: 没错。Mac 是全新的。我们知道，用当代技术——Apple II、IBM PC——我们可以触达早期创新者，因为他们会整晚不睡，学习如何使用自己的电脑。但我们永远触达不了大多数人。如果我们真的要把电脑带给数千万人，就需要一种技术，让这东西在使用上变得极其容易，同时又更强大，所以我们必须做一次断裂。我们就是必须这么做。我们想确保它足够伟大，因为这也许是我们任何人最后一次有机会做出一次彻底的断裂。我对 Macintosh 最终的样子非常满意。它会被证明是未来十年非常坚实的基础。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Let's go back to the predecessors of the Lisa and the Mac, to the beginning. How influential were your parents in your interest in computers?
units.translation_cn: 我们回到 Lisa 和 Mac 的前身，回到最开始。你的父母对你对电脑产生兴趣有多大影响？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: They encouraged my interests. My father was a machinist, and he was a sort of genius with his hands. He can fix anything and make it work and take any mechanical thing apart and get it back together. That was my first glimpse of it. I started to gravitate more toward electronics, and he used to get me things I could take apart and put back together. He was transferred to Palo Alto when I was five. That's how we ended up in the Valley.
units.translation_cn: 他们鼓励我的兴趣。我父亲是一名机械师，他的双手可以说有一种天才。他什么都能修好，让它重新运转；任何机械的东西，他都能拆开，再装回去。那是我第一次看见这种能力。我后来开始更多地被电子吸引，而他常常给我一些可以拆开再装回去的东西。我五岁时，他被调到帕洛阿尔托工作。我们就是这样来到硅谷的。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You had been adopted, hadn't you? How much of a factor in your life was that?
units.translation_cn: 你是被收养的，对吗？这在你的人生中有多大影响？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: You don't ever really know, do you?
units.translation_cn: 你永远不可能真正知道，不是吗？

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Did you try to find your biological parents?
units.translation_cn: 你尝试过去寻找自己的亲生父母吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I think it's quite a natural curiosity for adopted people to want to understand where certain traits come from. But I'm mostly an environmentalist. I think the way you are raised and your values and most of your world view come from the experiences you had as you grew up. But some things aren't accounted for that way. I think it's quite natural to have a curiosity about it. And I did.
units.translation_cn: 我认为，对被收养的人来说，想弄明白某些特质来自哪里，是一种相当自然的好奇。但总体上我是一个环境论者。我认为，你被如何养大，你的价值观，以及你大部分的世界观，都来自你成长过程中经历过的事情。但也有一些东西不能用这种方式解释。我认为，对此产生好奇是很自然的。而我确实也有这种好奇。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Were you successful in trying to find your natural parents?
units.translation_cn: 你在寻找亲生父母这件事上成功了吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: That's one area I really don't want to talk about.
units.translation_cn: 这是我真的不想谈的一个领域。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: The valley your parents moved to has since come to be known as Silicon Valley. What was it like growing up there?
units.translation_cn: 你父母搬去的那个山谷后来被称为硅谷。在那里长大是什么感觉？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It was the suburbs. It was like most suburbs in the U.S.: I grew up on a block with lots of kids. My mother taught me to read before I went to school, so I was pretty bored in school, and I turned into a little terror. You should have seen us in third grade. We basically destroyed our teacher. We would let snakes loose in the classroom and explode bombs. Things changed in the fourth grade, though. One of the saints in my life is this woman named Imogene Hill, who was a fourth-grade teacher who taught this advanced class. She got hip to my whole situation in about a month and kindled a passion in me for learning things. I learned more that year than I think I learned in any year in school. They wanted to put me in high school after that year, but my parents very wisely wouldn't let them.
units.translation_cn: 那是郊区。就像美国大多数郊区一样：我住的街区有很多孩子。我妈妈在我上学前就教会了我阅读，所以我在学校里相当无聊，后来就变成了一个小恶魔。你真该看看我们三年级时的样子。我们基本上把老师折腾垮了。我们会在教室里放蛇，还会引爆小炸弹。不过到了四年级，情况变了。我生命中的圣人之一，是一位叫伊莫金·希尔的女士。她是四年级老师，教一个高级班。她大约一个月就看懂了我的整个处境，并点燃了我对学习事物的热情。我那一年学到的东西，比我在学校任何一年学到的都多。那一年之后，他们想让我直接去上高中，但我父母非常明智地没有同意。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: But location had something to do with your interests, didn't it? How did Silicon Valley come to be?
units.translation_cn: 不过，地点和你的兴趣也有关系，对吗？硅谷是怎么形成的？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: The Valley is positioned strategically between two great universities, Berkeley and Stanford. Both of those universities attract not only lots of students but very good students and ones from all over the United States. They come here and fall in love with the area and they stay here. So there is a constant influx of new, bright human resources. Before World War Two, two Stanford graduates named Bill Hewlett and Dave Packard created a very innovative electronics company—Hewlett-Packard. Then the transistor was invented in 1948 by Bell Telephone Laboratories. One of the three coinventors of the transistor, William Shockley, decided to return to his home town of Palo Alto to start a little company called Shockley Labs or something. He brought with him about a dozen of the best and brightest physicists and chemists of his day. Little by little, people started breaking off and forming competitive companies, like those flowers or weeds that scatter seeds in hundreds of directions when you blow on them. And that's why the Valley is here today.
units.translation_cn: 这个山谷在地理上处在两所伟大大学之间：伯克利和斯坦福。这两所大学不仅吸引很多学生，也吸引非常优秀的学生，而且他们来自美国各地。他们来到这里，爱上这个地方，然后就留下来。所以这里一直有源源不断的、聪明的新人才涌入。二战前，有两位斯坦福毕业生，比尔·休利特和戴夫·帕卡德，创办了一家非常创新的电子公司——惠普。后来，晶体管在 1948 年由贝尔电话实验室发明。晶体管的三位共同发明者之一威廉·肖克利决定回到他的家乡帕洛阿尔托，创办一家叫肖克利实验室之类的小公司。他带来了当时最优秀、最聪明的一批物理学家和化学家，大约十几个人。慢慢地，人们开始分离出去，创办相互竞争的公司，就像那种花或杂草，你一吹，它的种子就向几百个方向散开。这就是硅谷今天在这里的原因。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What was your introduction to computers?
units.translation_cn: 你最早是怎么接触电脑的？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: A neighbor down the block named Larry Lang was an engineer at Hewlett-Packard. He spent a lot of time with me, teaching me stuff. The first computer I ever saw was at Hewlett-Packard. They used to invite maybe ten of us down every Tuesday night and give us lectures and let us work with a computer. I was maybe 12 the first time. I remember the night. They showed us one of their new desktop computers and let us play on it. I wanted one badly.
units.translation_cn: 街区里有一位邻居叫拉里·朗，是惠普的工程师。他花了很多时间陪我，教我东西。我见过的第一台电脑就在惠普。他们以前每周二晚上会邀请我们大概十个人过去，给我们上课，还让我们操作电脑。我第一次去时大概 12 岁。我还记得那个晚上。他们给我们看了一台新的桌面电脑，让我们在上面玩。我非常想要一台。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What was it about it that interested you? Did you have a sense of its potential?
units.translation_cn: 它的什么地方吸引了你？你当时意识到它的潜力了吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It wasn't anything like that. I just thought they were neat. I just wanted to mess around with one.
units.translation_cn: 不是那种感觉。我只是觉得它们很酷。我只是想摆弄一台。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You went to work for Hewlett-Packard. How did that happen?
units.translation_cn: 你后来去了惠普工作。那是怎么发生的？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: When I was 12 or 13, I wanted to build something and I needed some parts, so I picked up the phone and called Bill Hewlett—he was listed in the Palo Alto phone book. He answered the phone and he was real nice. He chatted with me for, like, 20 minutes. He didn't know me at all, but he ended up giving me some parts and he got me a job that summer working at Hewlett-Packard on the line, assembling frequency counters. Assembling may be too strong. I was putting in screws. It didn't matter; I was in heaven. I remember my first day, expressing my complete enthusiasm and bliss at being at Hewlett-Packard for the summer to my supervisor, a guy named Chris, telling him that my favorite thing in the whole world was electronics. I asked him what his favorite thing to do was and he looked at me and said, "To fuck!" [Laughs] I learned a lot that summer.
units.translation_cn: 我十二三岁的时候，想做点东西，需要一些零件，于是我拿起电话打给比尔·休利特——他的号码就在帕洛阿尔托电话簿里。他接了电话，而且非常友善。他和我聊了大概 20 分钟。他完全不认识我，但最后还是给了我一些零件，还让我那个夏天在惠普生产线上找了份工作，组装频率计。说「组装」可能有点夸张，我其实是在拧螺丝。但这没关系，我简直像在天堂。我记得第一天，我对能在惠普过暑假表现出完全的热情和幸福感。我对我的主管——一个叫克里斯的人——说，我全世界最喜欢的东西就是电子。我问他最喜欢做什么，他看着我说：「做爱！」[笑] 那个夏天我学到了很多。
units.translator_note: 原文粗口按语义直译；frequency counter 译为「频率计」。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: At what point did you meet Steve Wozniak?
units.translation_cn: 你是在什么时候认识史蒂夫·沃兹尼亚克的？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I met Woz when I was 13, at a friend's garage. He was about 18. He was, like, the first person I met who knew more electronics than I did at that point. We became good friends, because we shared an interest in computers and we had a sense of humor. We pulled all kinds of pranks together.
units.translation_cn: 我 13 岁时在一个朋友的车库里认识了沃兹。他大概 18 岁。他可以说是我当时遇到的第一个电子知识比我还多的人。我们成了好朋友，因为我们都对电脑感兴趣，也都有幽默感。我们一起搞过各种恶作剧。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: For instance?
units.translation_cn: 比如说？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: [Grins] Normal stuff. Like making a huge flag with a giant one of these on it [gives the finger]. The idea was that we would unfurl it in the middle of a school graduation. Then there was the time Wozniak made something that looked and sounded like a bomb and took it to the school cafeteria. We also went into the blue-box business together.
units.translation_cn: [咧嘴笑] 普通的那些事。比如做一面巨大的旗子，上面画着一个巨大的这种手势[竖中指]。我们的想法是在学校毕业典礼进行到一半时把它展开。还有一次，沃兹尼亚克做了一个看起来像炸弹、声音也像炸弹的东西，把它带到了学校餐厅。我们还一起做过蓝盒子生意。
units.translator_note: blue-box 指可模拟电话系统信号、用于免费长途通话的非法设备。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Those were illegal devices that allowed free long-distance phone calls, weren't they?
units.translation_cn: 那是可以免费打长途电话的非法设备，对吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Mm-hm. The famous story about the boxes is when Woz called the Vatican and told them he was Henry Kissinger. They had someone going to wake the Pope up in the middle of the night before they figured out it wasn't really Kissinger.
units.translation_cn: 嗯。关于那些盒子最有名的故事，是沃兹打电话到梵蒂冈，告诉他们自己是亨利·基辛格。他们差点派人半夜去叫醒教皇，后来才发现那并不是真的基辛格。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Did you get into trouble for any of those things?
units.translation_cn: 你因为这些事惹过麻烦吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Well, I was thrown out of school a few times.
units.translation_cn: 嗯，我有几次被学校赶出去。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Were you then, or have you ever been, a computer nerd?
units.translation_cn: 你当时是，或者曾经是，一个电脑书呆子吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I wasn't completely in any one world for too long. There was so much else going on. Between my sophomore and junior years, I got stoned for the first time; I discovered Shakespeare, Dylan Thomas and all that classic stuff. I read Moby Dick and went back as a junior taking creative-writing classes. By the time I was a senior, I'd gotten permission to spend about half my time at Stanford, taking classes.
units.translation_cn: 我从来没有在任何一个世界里待得太彻底、太久。当时还有太多别的事情在发生。高二到高三之间，我第一次吸了大麻；我发现了莎士比亚、迪伦·托马斯，以及那些经典作品。我读了《白鲸》，高三时又回去上创意写作课。到高四时，我获得许可，可以把大约一半时间花在斯坦福上课。
units.translator_note: sophomore/junior/senior 按美国高中年级语境处理；got stoned 译为「吸了大麻」。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Was Wozniak obsessed at certain periods?
units.translation_cn: 沃兹尼亚克在某些时期也很痴迷吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: [Laughs] Yes, but not just with computers. I think Woz was in a world that nobody understood. No one shared his interests, and he was a little ahead of his time. It was very lonely for him. He's driven from inner sights rather than external expectations of him, so he survived OK. Woz and I are different in most ways, but there are some ways in which we're the same, and we're very close in those ways. We're sort of like two planets in their own orbits that every so often intersect. It wasn't just computers, either. Woz and I very much liked Bob Dylan's poetry, and we spent a lot of time thinking about a lot of that stuff. This was California. You could get LSD fresh made from Stanford. You could sleep on the beach at night with your girlfriend. California has a sense of experimentation and a sense of openness—openness to new possibilities. Besides Dylan, I was interested in Eastern mysticism, which hit the shores at about the same time. When I went to college at Reed, in Oregon, there was a constant flow of people stopping by, from Timothy Leary and Richard Alpert to Gary Snyder. There was a constant flow of intellectual questioning about the truth of life. That was a time when every college student in this country read Be Here Now and Diet for a Small Planet—there were about ten books. You'd be hard pressed to find those books on too many college campuses today. I'm not saying it's better or worse; it's just different—very different. In Search of Excellence [the book about business practices] has taken the place of Be Here Now.
units.translation_cn: [笑] 是的，但不只是痴迷电脑。我觉得沃兹处在一个没人理解的世界里。没有人分享他的兴趣，而且他有点超前于时代。那对他来说非常孤独。他是由内在的洞见驱动，而不是由外界对他的期待驱动，所以他挺过来了。沃兹和我在大多数方面都不同，但也有一些方面我们是一样的，而我们在这些方面非常亲近。我们有点像两颗各自沿着轨道运行的行星，每隔一段时间就会相交一次。也不只是电脑。沃兹和我都非常喜欢鲍勃·迪伦的诗歌，我们花了很多时间思考那些东西。这是加州。你可以拿到斯坦福刚做出来的新鲜 LSD。晚上可以和女朋友睡在海滩上。加州有一种实验精神，也有一种开放感——对新可能性的开放。除了迪伦，我还对东方神秘主义感兴趣，而它大约也是在同一时期登陆美国西海岸的。我去俄勒冈的里德学院读书时，经常有人来来往往，从蒂莫西·利里、理查德·阿尔珀特到加里·斯奈德。那时关于生命真相的智性追问一直在流动。那是一个这个国家每个大学生都会读《活在当下》和《小星球饮食》的时代——大概有十本这样的书。今天你很难在太多大学校园里找到这些书。我不是说那更好或更坏；只是不同——非常不同。《追求卓越》取代了《活在当下》。
units.translator_note: 书名按常见中文理解处理，在保留语义的同时适合中文阅读。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: In retrospect, how did that influence what you're doing now?
units.translation_cn: 回头看，那对你现在所做的事情有什么影响？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: The whole period had a huge influence. As it was clear that the Sixties were over, it was also clear that a lot of the people who had gone through the Sixties ended up not really accomplishing what they set out to accomplish, and because they had thrown their discipline to the wind, they didn't have much to fall back on. Many of my friends have ended up ingrained with the idealism of that period but also with a certain practicality, a cautiousness about ending up working behind the counter in a natural-food store when they are 45, which is what they saw happen to some of their older friends. It's not that that is bad in and of itself, but it's bad if that's not what you really wanted to do.
units.translation_cn: 整个那个时期影响巨大。六十年代结束这一点变得很清楚时，另一件事也很清楚：很多经历过六十年代的人，最终并没有真正完成他们原本打算完成的事情。而因为他们把纪律抛到了九霄云外，所以他们没有太多可以依靠的东西。我有很多朋友身上都深深带着那个时期的理想主义，但同时也带着某种现实感，一种谨慎：他们不想在 45 岁时最后站在天然食品店的柜台后面工作，而这正是他们看到一些年长朋友遭遇的事情。这本身并不坏，但如果那不是你真正想做的事，那就不好。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: After Reed, you returned to Silicon Valley and answered a now-famous ad that boasted, "Have fun and make money."
units.translation_cn: 里德之后，你回到了硅谷，并回应了一则后来很有名的招聘广告，上面写着：「玩得开心，赚到钱。」
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气和内容更像 Playboy 提问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Right. I decided I wanted to travel, but I was lacking the necessary funds. I came back down to get a job. I was looking in the paper and there was this ad that said, yes, "Have fun and make money." I called. It was Atari. I had never had a job before other than the one when I was a kid. By some stroke of luck, they called me up the next day and hired me.
units.translation_cn: 对。我当时决定想去旅行，但缺少必要的资金。我回来找工作。我在报纸上看到一则广告，上面写着，是的，「玩得开心，赚到钱」。我打了电话。那是雅达利。除了小时候那份工作之外，我以前从来没有正式工作过。很幸运，他们第二天就打电话给我，雇用了我。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: That must have been at Atari's earliest stage.
units.translation_cn: 那一定是在雅达利最早期的时候吧。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气更像 Playboy 追问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I was, like, employee number 40. It was a very small company. They had made Pong and two other games. My first job was helping a guy named Don work on a basketball game, which was a disaster. There was this basketball game, and somebody was working on a hockey game. They were trying to model all their games after simple field sports at that time, because Pong was such a success.
units.translation_cn: 我大概是第 40 号员工。公司非常小。他们做过 Pong 和另外两款游戏。我的第一份工作是帮一个叫唐的人做篮球游戏，结果是一场灾难。有个篮球游戏，还有个人在做曲棍球游戏。当时他们试图把所有游戏都模仿简单的场地运动来建模，因为 Pong 太成功了。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You never lost sight of the reason for the job: to earn money so you could travel.
units.translation_cn: 你从来没有忘记这份工作的目的：赚钱，这样你就可以去旅行。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Atari had shipped a bunch of games to Europe and they had some engineering defects in them, and I figured out how to fix them, but it was necessary for somebody to go over there and actually do the fixing. I volunteered to go and asked to take a leave of absence when I was there. They let me do it. I ended up in Switzerland and moved from Zurich to New Delhi. I spent some time in India.
units.translation_cn: 雅达利向欧洲发货了一批游戏，但其中有一些工程缺陷，我想出了怎么修好它们，但必须有人去那里实际做维修工作。我主动报名去，并在逗留期间申请了停薪留职。他们同意了。我最后到了瑞士，从苏黎世搬到了新德里。我在印度待了一段时间。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Where you shaved your head.
units.translation_cn: 你就在那里剃了光头。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: That's not quite the way it happened. I was walking around in the Himalayas and I stumbled onto this thing that turned out to be a religious festival. There was a baba, a holy man, who was the holy man of this particular festival, with his large group of followers. I could smell good food. I hadn't been fortunate enough to smell good food for a long time, so I wandered up to pay my respects and eat some lunch. For some reason, this baba, upon seeing me sitting there eating, immediately walked over to me and sat down and burst out laughing. He didn't speak much English and I spoke a little Hindi, but he tried to carry on a conversation and he was just rolling on the ground with laughter. Then he grabbed my arm and took me up this mountain trail. It was a little funny, because here were hundreds of Indians who had traveled for thousands of miles to hang out with this guy for ten seconds and I stumble in for something to eat and he's dragging me up this mountain path. We get to the top of this mountain half an hour later and there's this little well and pond at the top of this mountain, and he dunks my head in the water and pulls out a razor from his pocket and starts to shave my head. I'm completely stunned. I'm 19 years old, in a foreign country, up in the Himalayas, and here is this bizarre Indian baba who has just dragged me away from the rest of the crowd, shaving my head atop this mountain peak. I'm still not sure why he did it.
units.translation_cn: 事情并不是完全那样发生的。我当时正在喜马拉雅山脉徒步，偶然碰到了一个后来发现是宗教节日的东西。那里有一位巴巴，也就是一位圣者，是这个特定节日的主人，身边跟着一大群信徒。我闻到了美食的香味。我很久没有幸运地闻到美食的香味了，所以我走过去致敬并吃了午饭。出于某种原因，这位巴巴看到我坐在那里吃饭，立刻走过来坐下，然后笑得在地上打滚。他不太会说英语，我的印地语也只会一点，但他试图继续对话，笑得在地上打滚。然后他抓住我的胳膊，带我上了山间小路。这有点好笑，因为这里有数百名印度人，他们旅行了上千里，只为了和这个人待上十秒钟，而我偶然走进来吃点东西，他却拖着我上了山。我们半小时后到了山顶，山顶上有小水井和水塘，他把我的头按进水里，从口袋里掏出一把剃刀，开始给我剃头。我完全惊呆了。我 19 岁，在一个外国，在喜马拉雅山脉上，而这里有个奇怪的印度巴巴，刚刚把我从人群里拖走，在山顶上给我剃头。我至今也不确定他为什么要这样做。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What did you do when you came back?
units.translation_cn: 你回来之后做了什么？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Coming back was more of a culture shock than going. Well, Atari called me up and wanted me to go back to work there. I didn't really want to, but eventually they persuaded me to go back as a consultant. Wozniak and I were hanging out. He took me to some Homebrew Computer Club meetings, where computer hobbyists compared notes and stuff. I didn't find them all that exciting, but some of them were fun. Wozniak went religiously.
units.translation_cn: 回来比去的时候更有文化冲击。嗯，雅达利打电话给我，想让我回去工作。我不太想回去，但最终他们说服了我回去当顾问。沃兹尼亚克和我常常待在一起。他带我去了一些家酿计算机俱乐部聚会，在那里电脑爱好者们交流心得什么的。我觉得并不是都很精彩，但有一些挺有趣的。沃兹尼亚克每次都去。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What was the thinking about computers then? Why were you interested?
units.translation_cn: 当时对电脑是怎么想的？你们为什么感兴趣？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: The clubs were based around a computer kit called the Altair. It was so amazing to all of us that somebody had actually come up with a way to build a computer you could own yourself. That had never been possible. Remember, when we were in high school, neither of us had access to a computer mainframe. We had to drive somewhere and have some large company take a benevolent attitude toward us and let us use the computer. But now, for the first time, you could actually buy a computer. The Altair was a kit that came out around 1975 and sold for less than $400. Even though it was relatively inexpensive, not everyone could afford one. That's how the computer clubs started. People would band together and eventually become a club.
units.translation_cn: 那些俱乐部是围绕一种叫 Altair 的电脑套件建立的。对我们所有人来说，竟然有人想出了办法做出了一台可以属于自己的电脑，这太惊人了。那是从未有过的。记住，我们上高中时，我们两个人都没有机会接触到电脑主机。我们必须开车去某个地方，让某个大公司以仁慈的态度对待我们，让我们使用电脑。但现在，第一次，你可以真正买到一台电脑了。Altair 是一种 1975 年左右推出的套件，售价不到 400 美元。尽管相对便宜，但不是每个人都买得起。这就是计算机俱乐部开始的原因。人们会联合起来，最终成为一个俱乐部。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What would you do with your makeshift computers?
units.translation_cn: 你们用那些临时拼凑的电脑做什么？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: At that time, there were no graphics. It was all alphanumerics, and I used to be fascinated with the programming, simple programming. On the very early versions of computer kits, you didn't even type; you threw switches that signaled characters.
units.translation_cn: 那时候没有图形。全部都是字母数字，而我过去常常被编程所吸引，简单的编程。在非常早期的电脑套件版本上，你甚至不用打字；你拨动开关来发出字符信号。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: The Altair, then, presented the concept of a home computer.
units.translation_cn: 那么，Altair 提出了家用电脑的概念。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气更像 Playboy 小结；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It was just sort of a computer that you could own. They really didn't know what to do with it. The first thing that they did was to put languages on it, so you could write some programs. People didn't start to apply them for practical things until a year or two later, and then it was simple things, like bookkeeping.
units.translation_cn: 它就是一种你可以拥有的电脑。他们真的不知道用它做什么。他们做的第一件事是在上面装语言，这样你就可以写一些程序。人们过了一两年才开始把它们应用到实际的事情上，而且都是简单的事情，比如记账。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: And you decided you could do the Altair one better.
units.translation_cn: 而你决定你可以做得比 Altair 更好。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It sort of just happened. I was working a lot at Atari at night and I used to let Woz in. Atari put out a game called Gran Track, the first driving game with a steering wheel to drive it. Woz was a Gran Track addict. He would put great quantities of quarters into these games to play them, so I would just let him in at night and let him onto the production floor and he would play Gran Track all night long. When I came up against a stumbling block on a project, I would get Woz to take a break from his road rally for ten minutes and come and help me. He puttered around on some things, too.
