And at one point, he designed a computer terminal with video on it. At a later date, he ended up buying a microprocessor and hooking it up to the terminal and made what was to become the Apple I. Woz and I laid out the circuit board ourselves. That was basically it.
units.translation_cn: 这有点像是自然而然发生的。我当时经常晚上在雅达利工作，也常常让沃兹进来。雅达利推出过一款叫 Gran Track 的游戏，是第一款用方向盘来驾驶的赛车游戏。沃兹是 Gran Track 迷。他会往这些游戏机里投大量硬币来玩，所以我晚上就让他进来，让他到生产车间去，他就整晚玩 Gran Track。当我在某个项目上遇到障碍时，我会让沃兹从他的公路拉力赛里休息十分钟，过来帮我。他自己也会摆弄一些东西。有一次，他设计了一个带视频显示的计算机终端。后来，他买了一个微处理器，把它接到那个终端上，于是做出了后来成为 Apple I 的东西。沃兹和我自己画出了电路板。基本就是这样。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Again, the idea was just to do it?
units.translation_cn: 所以，又一次，想法只是把它做出来？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Yeah, sure. And to be able to show it off to your friends.
units.translation_cn: 是的，当然。还可以拿去向朋友炫耀。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What triggered the next step—manufacturing and selling them to make money?
units.translation_cn: 是什么触发了下一步——开始制造并销售它们来赚钱？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Woz and I raised $1300 by selling my VW bus and his Hewlett-Packard calculator to finance them. A guy who started one of the first computer stores told us he could sell them if we could make them. It had not dawned on us until then.
units.translation_cn: 沃兹和我卖掉了我的大众小巴和他的惠普计算器，筹到了 1300 美元来为它们融资。一个开了最早一批计算机商店的人告诉我们，如果我们能做出来，他就能卖出去。直到那时，我们才意识到这一点。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How did you and Wozniak work together?
units.translation_cn: 你和沃兹尼亚克是怎么合作的？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: He designed most of it. I helped on the memory part and I helped when we decided to turn it into a product. Woz isn't great at turning things into products, but he's really a brilliant designer.
units.translation_cn: 大部分都是他设计的。我在存储器部分帮了忙，也在我们决定把它变成产品时帮了忙。沃兹并不擅长把东西变成产品，但他真的是一位杰出的设计者。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: The Apple I was for hobbyists?
units.translation_cn: Apple I 是给爱好者用的吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Completely. We sold only about 150 of them, ever. It wasn't that big a deal, but we made about $95,000 and I started to see it as a business besides something to do. Apple I was just a printed circuit board. There was no case, there was no power supply; it wasn't much of a product yet. It was just a printed circuit board. You had to go out and buy transformers for it. You had to buy your own keyboard [laughs].
units.translation_cn: 完全是。我们总共只卖了大约 150 台。那并不是什么大事，但我们赚了大约 9.5 万美元，我开始把它看作一门生意，而不只是可以做的一件事。Apple I 只是一块印刷电路板。没有外壳，没有电源；它还算不上什么完整产品。它就是一块印刷电路板。你必须自己去买变压器。你还得自己买键盘[笑]。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Did you and Wozniak have a vision once things started rolling? Were you both thinking about how big it could get and how computers would be able to change the world?
units.translation_cn: 事情开始运转起来之后，你和沃兹尼亚克有过愿景吗？你们两个人有没有想过它会变得多大，计算机又会如何改变世界？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: No, not particularly. Neither of us had any idea that this would go anywhere. Woz is motivated by figuring things out. He concentrated more on the engineering and proceeded to do one of his most brilliant pieces of work, which was the disk drive, another key engineering feat that made the Apple II a possibility. I was trying to build the company—trying to find out what a company was. I don't think it would have happened without Woz and I don't think it would have happened without me.
units.translation_cn: 没有，并没有特别想过。我们两个人都完全不知道这件事会发展到哪里。沃兹的动力来自把事情弄明白。他更专注于工程，并且完成了他最杰出的作品之一，也就是磁盘驱动器；那是另一个关键工程成就，使 Apple II 成为可能。而我当时在努力建立公司——努力弄清楚一家公司到底是什么。我不认为没有沃兹这件事会发生，也不认为没有我这件事会发生。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What happened to the partnership as time went on?
units.translation_cn: 随着时间推移，你们的合作关系发生了什么变化？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: The main thing was that Woz was never really interested in Apple as a company. He was just sort of interested in getting the Apple II on a printed circuit board so he could have one and be able to carry it to his computer club without having the wires break on the way. He had done that and decided to go on to other things. He had other ideas.
units.translation_cn: 最主要的是，沃兹其实从来没有真正对苹果作为一家公司感兴趣。他只是有点想把 Apple II 做到一块印刷电路板上，这样他自己就能拥有一台，并且可以把它带到计算机俱乐部，而不用担心路上把线弄断。他完成了那件事之后，就决定去做别的事情。他还有其他想法。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Such as the US Festival rock concert and computer show, where he lost something like $10,000,000.
units.translation_cn: 比如 US Festival 摇滚音乐会和计算机展，他在那里亏了大约 1000 万美元。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气更像 Playboy 举例追问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Well, I thought the US Festival was a little crazy, but Woz believed very strongly in it.
units.translation_cn: 嗯，我觉得 US Festival 有点疯狂，但沃兹非常相信它。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How is it between the two of you now?
units.translation_cn: 你们两个人现在关系怎么样？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: When you work with somebody that close and you go through experiences like the ones we went through, there's a bond in life. Whatever hassles you have, there is a bond. And even though he may not be your best friend as time goes on, there's still something that transcends even friendship, in a way. Woz is living his own life now. He hasn't been around Apple for about five years. But what he did will go down in history. He's going around speaking to a lot of computer events now. He likes that.
units.translation_cn: 当你和某个人合作得那么紧密，并且一起经历了我们经历过的那些事情，生命中就会形成一种纽带。不管你们之间有多少麻烦，那种纽带都还在那里。即使随着时间推移，他也许不再是你最好的朋友，某种意义上，仍然有一种东西甚至超越了友谊。沃兹现在过着他自己的生活。他已经大约五年没怎么在苹果出现了。但他所做的事情会被载入历史。现在他到很多计算机活动上演讲。他喜欢那样。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: The two of you went on to create the Apple II, which actually started the computer revolution. How did that occur?
units.translation_cn: 你们两个人后来创造了 Apple II，而它实际上开启了计算机革命。那是怎么发生的？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It wasn't just us. We brought in other people. Wozniak still did the logic of the Apple II, which certainly is a large part of it, but there were some other key parts. The power supply was really a key. The case was really a key. The real jump with the Apple II was that it was a finished product. It was the first computer that you could buy that wasn't a kit. It was fully assembled and had its own case and its own keyboard, and you could really sit down and start to use it. And that was the breakthrough of the Apple II: that it looked like a real product.
units.translation_cn: 那不只是我们两个人。我们还引入了其他人。Apple II 的逻辑部分仍然是沃兹尼亚克做的，那当然是其中很大的一部分，但还有其他一些关键部分。电源非常关键。外壳也非常关键。Apple II 真正的跃迁在于，它是一个成品。它是第一台你可以买到的、不是套件的计算机。它已经完全组装好，有自己的外壳，也有自己的键盘，你真的可以坐下来就开始使用它。这就是 Apple II 的突破：它看起来像一个真正的产品。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Was the initial market hobbyists?
units.translation_cn: 最初的市场是爱好者吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: The difference was that you didn't have to be a hardware hobbyist with the Apple II. You could be a software hobbyist. That was one of the key breakthroughs with the Apple II: realizing that there were a whole lot more people who wanted to play with a computer, just like Woz and me, than there were people who could build their own. That's what the Apple II was all about. Still, the first year, we sold only 3000 or 4000.
units.translation_cn: 区别在于，使用 Apple II 时，你不必是硬件爱好者。你可以是软件爱好者。这是 Apple II 的关键突破之一：意识到有很多很多人想像沃兹和我一样玩电脑，但真正能自己造电脑的人要少得多。Apple II 的意义就在这里。不过，第一年我们也只卖了三四千台。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Even that sounds like a lot for a few guys who barely knew what they were doing.
units.translation_cn: 即使这样，对几个几乎不知道自己在做什么的人来说，听起来也已经很多了。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气更像 Playboy 追问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: It was giant! We did about $200,000 when our business was in the garage, in 1976. In 1977, about $7,000,000 in business. I mean, it was phenomenal! And in 1978, we did $17,000,000. In 1979, we did $47,000,000. That's when we all really sensed that this was just going through the rafters. In 1980, we did $117,000,000. In 1981, we did $335,000,000. In 1982, we did $583,000,000. In 1983, we did $985,000,000, I think. This year, it will be a billion and a half.
units.translation_cn: 那是巨大的！1976 年，我们的业务还在车库里时，做了大约 20 万美元。1977 年，大约 700 万美元。我是说，那太惊人了！1978 年，我们做了 1700 万美元。1979 年，我们做了 4700 万美元。那时我们都真正感觉到，这件事正在冲破屋顶。1980 年，我们做了 1.17 亿美元。1981 年，3.35 亿美元。1982 年，5.83 亿美元。1983 年，我想是 9.85 亿美元。今年会达到 15 亿美元。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You don't forget those numbers.
units.translation_cn: 这些数字你不会忘。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Well, they're just yardsticks, you know. The neatest thing was, by 1979, I was able to walk into classrooms that had 15 Apple computers and see the kids using them. And those are the kinds of things that are really the milestones.
units.translation_cn: 嗯，它们只是标尺，你知道。最棒的是，到 1979 年，我可以走进那些有 15 台苹果电脑的教室，看见孩子们在使用它们。而这些事情，才是真正的里程碑。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Which brings us full circle to your latest milestones, the Mac and your protracted shoot-out with IBM. In this Interview, you've repeatedly sounded as if there really are only two of you left in the field. But although the two of you account for something like 60 percent of the market, can you just write off the other 40 percent—the Radio Shacks, DECs, Epsons, et al.—as insignificant? More important, are you ignoring your potentially biggest rival, A.T.&T.?
units.translation_cn: 这又把我们带回到你最新的里程碑：Mac，以及你和 IBM 旷日持久的对决。在这次访谈中，你反复听起来像是在说，这个领域里实际上只剩下你们两家了。但尽管你们两家大约占了市场的 60%，你能就这样把另外 40%——Radio Shack、DEC、Epson 等等——都视为无足轻重吗？更重要的是，你是不是忽略了你们潜在的最大对手 AT&T？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: A.T.&T.. is absolutely going to be in the business. There is a major transformation in the company that's taking place right now. A.T.&T. is changing from a subsidized and regulated service-oriented company to a free-market, competitive-marketing technology company. A.T.&T.'s products per se have never been of the highest quality. All you have to do is go look at their telephones. They're somewhat of an embarrassment. But they do possess great technology in their research labs. Their challenge is to learn how to commercialize that technology. Also, they have to learn about consumer marketing. I think that they will do both of those things, but it's going to take them years.
units.translation_cn: AT&T 绝对会进入这个行业。那家公司现在正在发生一场重大转型。AT&T 正在从一家受补贴、受监管、以服务为导向的公司，转变为一家自由市场中竞争营销型的技术公司。AT&T 的产品本身从来都不是最高质量的。你只要去看看他们的电话就知道了。那多少有点令人尴尬。但他们的研究实验室确实拥有很强大的技术。他们的挑战是学习如何把那些技术商业化。此外，他们还必须学习消费者营销。我认为他们会做到这两件事，但这会花上好几年。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Are you writing them off as a threat?
units.translation_cn: 你是说可以把他们作为威胁排除掉吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I don't think they're going to be a giant factor in the next 24 months, but they will learn.
units.translation_cn: 我不认为他们会在接下来的 24 个月里成为一个巨大的因素，但他们会学会的。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What about Radio Shack?
units.translation_cn: Radio Shack 呢？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Radio Shack is totally out of the picture. They have missed the boat. Radio Shack tried to squeeze the computer into their model of retailing, which in my opinion often meant selling second-rate products or low-end products in a surplus-store environment. The sophistication of the computer buyer passed Radio Shack by without their really realizing it. Their market shares dropped through the floor. I don't anticipate that they're going to recover and again become a major player.
units.translation_cn: Radio Shack 已经完全出局了。他们错过了这班车。Radio Shack 试图把计算机塞进自己的零售模式里，而在我看来，那通常意味着在一种折扣余货商店式的环境里销售二流产品或低端产品。计算机买家的成熟程度已经越过了 Radio Shack，而他们自己甚至没有真正意识到这一点。他们的市场份额跌穿了地板。我不认为他们会恢复过来，并再次成为主要玩家。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How about Xerox? Texas Instruments? DEC? Wang?
units.translation_cn: 施乐、德州仪器、DEC、王安呢？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Xerox is out of the business. T.I. is doing nowhere near their expectations. As to some of the others, the large companies, like DEC and Wang, can sell to their installed bases. They can sell personal computers as advanced terminals, but that business is going to dwindle.
units.translation_cn: 施乐已经退出这个业务了。德州仪器的表现远远达不到他们的预期。至于其他一些大公司，比如 DEC 和王安，它们可以卖给自己的既有客户群。它们可以把个人电脑当作高级终端来销售，但那项业务会逐渐萎缩。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How about the low-priced computers: Commodore and Atari?
units.translation_cn: 低价电脑呢？Commodore 和 Atari？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I consider those a brochure for why you should buy an Apple II or Macintosh. I think people have already determined that the sub-$500 computers don't do very much. They either tease people to want more or frustrate people completely.
units.translation_cn: 我把它们看作一份宣传册，说明为什么你应该买 Apple II 或 Macintosh。我认为人们已经看清楚，500 美元以下的电脑做不了太多事情。它们要么引诱人想要更多，要么让人完全沮丧。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What about some of the smaller portables?
units.translation_cn: 那些更小型的便携电脑呢？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: They are OK if you're a reporter and trying to take notes on the run. But for the average person, they're really not that useful, and there's not all that software for them, either. By the time you get your software done, a new one comes out with a slightly bigger display and your software is obsolete. So nobody is writing any software for them. Wait till we do it—the power of a Macintosh in something the size of a book!
units.translation_cn: 如果你是记者，想一边跑动一边记笔记，它们还可以。但对普通人来说，它们真的没有那么有用，而且也没有那么多软件可用。等你把软件做出来，新的机型又出来了，显示屏稍微大一点，你的软件就过时了。所以没人为它们写软件。等我们来做吧——把 Macintosh 的能力放进一本书大小的东西里！

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What about Epson and some of the Japanese computer makers?
units.translation_cn: Epson 和一些日本计算机制造商呢？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I've said it before: The Japanese have hit the shores like dead fish. They're just like dead fish washing up on the shores. The Epson has been a failure in this market place.
units.translation_cn: 我以前说过：日本人像死鱼一样冲上了海岸。他们就像死鱼被冲上海滩。Epson 在这个市场上已经失败了。
units.translator_note: 保留原文尖锐比喻 dead fish washing up on the shores。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Like computers, the automobile industry was an American industry that we almost lost to the Japanese. There is a lot of talk about American semiconductor companies' losing ground to Japanese. How will you keep the edge?
units.translation_cn: 和计算机一样，汽车工业曾经也是一个美国工业，但我们几乎把它输给了日本。也有很多人在谈美国半导体公司正在输给日本。你们将如何保持优势？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Japan's very interesting. Some people think it copies things. I don't think that anymore. I think what they do is reinvent things. They will get something that's already been invented and study it until they thoroughly understand it. In some cases, they understand it better than the original inventor. Out of that understanding, they will reinvent it in a more refined second-generation version. That strategy works only when what they're working with isn't changing very much—the stereo industry and the automobile industry are two examples. When the target is moving quickly, they find it very difficult, because that reinvention cycle takes a few years. As long as the definition of what a personal computer is keeps changing at the rate that it is, they will have a very hard time. Once the rate of change slows down, the Japanese will bring all of their strengths to bear on this market, because they absolutely want to dominate the computer business; there's no question about that. They see that as a national priority. We think that in four to five years, the Japanese will finally figure out how to build a decent computer. And if we're going to keep this industry one in which America leads, we have four years to become world-class manufacturers. Our manufacturing technology has to equal or surpass that of the Japanese.
units.translation_cn: 日本非常有意思。有些人认为它只是复制东西。我现在不再这么认为。我认为他们做的是重新发明。他们会拿到一个已经被发明出来的东西，然后研究它，直到彻底理解它。在某些情况下，他们甚至比最初的发明者理解得更透彻。基于这种理解，他们会以更精炼的第二代版本重新发明它。只有当他们面对的对象变化不大时，这种策略才有效——音响行业和汽车行业就是两个例子。当目标移动得很快时，他们就会发现这非常困难，因为这种重新发明的周期需要几年。只要“个人电脑是什么”这个定义还在以现在这样的速度变化，他们就会非常吃力。一旦变化速度放慢，日本人就会把他们所有优势都投入这个市场，因为他们绝对想主导计算机业务；这一点毫无疑问。他们把这看作国家优先事项。我们认为，四到五年后，日本人最终会弄明白如何造出一台像样的计算机。而如果我们想让这个行业继续由美国领先，我们就有四年时间成为世界级制造商。我们的制造技术必须等于或超过日本。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: How do you plan to accomplish that?
units.translation_cn: 你们打算如何做到这一点？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: At the time we designed Macintosh, we also designed a machine to build the machine. We spent $20,000,000 building the computer industry's most automated factory. But that's not enough. Rather than take seven years to write off our factory, as most companies would do, we're writing it off in two. We will throw it away at the end of 1985 and build our second one, and we will write that off in two years and throw that away, so that three years from now, we'll be on to our third automated factory. That's the only way we can learn fast enough.
units.translation_cn: 在我们设计 Macintosh 的同时，我们也设计了一台用来制造这台机器的机器。我们花了 2000 万美元，建造了计算机行业自动化程度最高的工厂。但这还不够。大多数公司会用七年时间摊销一座工厂，而我们不是，我们用两年就把它摊销掉。到 1985 年底，我们会把它扔掉，建第二座工厂；然后我们也会用两年把第二座工厂摊销掉，再把它扔掉。这样，三年后，我们就会进入第三座自动化工厂。只有这样，我们才能学得足够快。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: It's not all competition with the Japanese: You buy your disk drives from Sony, for instance.
units.translation_cn: 这并不全是和日本竞争：比如说，你们的磁盘驱动器就是从索尼购买的。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气明显是 Playboy 追问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: We buy many of our components from the Japanese. We're the largest user in the world of microprocessors, of high-technology RAM chips, of disk drives, of keyboards. We save a ton of energy not having to make and design floppy-disk drives or microprocessors that we can spend on software.
units.translation_cn: 我们的许多零部件都是从日本购买的。我们是世界上最大的微处理器、高科技 RAM 芯片、磁盘驱动器和键盘用户。我们不用自己制造和设计软盘驱动器或微处理器，可以省下大量精力，把它投入到软件上。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Let's talk about software. What are the revolutionary changes in software development as you've seen it in the past few years?
units.translation_cn: 我们来谈谈软件。就你过去几年所看到的情况而言，软件开发中发生了哪些革命性的变化？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Certainly, the earlier programming, getting a programming language on a microprocessor chip, was a real breakthrough. VisiCalc was a breakthrough, because that was the first real use of computers in business, where business people could see tangible benefits of using one. Before that, you had to program your own applications, and the number of people who want to program is a small fraction—one percent. Coupled with VisiCalc, the ability to graph things, graph information, was important, and so was Lotus.
units.translation_cn: 当然，早期的编程——把一种编程语言放到微处理器芯片上——是真正的突破。VisiCalc 是一个突破，因为那是计算机在商业中第一次真正的应用，商务人士可以看到使用计算机带来的实际好处。在那之前，你必须自己编写应用程序，而愿意编程的人只是很小一部分——也许只有百分之一。与 VisiCalc 相结合，把东西图形化、把信息图表化的能力也很重要，Lotus 也是如此。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: We're dropping a lot of brand names with which people may not be familiar. Please explain them.
units.translation_cn: 我们提到了很多人们可能不熟悉的品牌名。请解释一下它们。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气明显像 Playboy 追问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: What Lotus did was combine a good spread sheet and graphics program. The word-processing and data-base parts of Lotus are certainly not the most robust that one can purchase. The real key to Lotus was that it combined spread sheet and graphics in one program, so you could go between them very rapidly. The next breakthrough is happening now, thanks to the Macintosh, which brought that Lisa technology down to an affordable price. There exists, and there will be more, revolutionary software there. You generally want to truly evaluate a breakthrough a few years after it happens.
units.translation_cn: Lotus 所做的，是把一个不错的电子表格程序和图形程序结合起来。Lotus 的文字处理和数据库部分，当然并不是市面上可以买到的最强大的那种。Lotus 真正的关键在于，它把电子表格和图形放进了同一个程序，所以你可以在二者之间非常快速地切换。下一个突破现在正在发生，这要归功于 Macintosh，它把 Lisa 的那套技术降到了一个负担得起的价格。在那里已经有，也将会有更多革命性的软件。通常你需要在一个突破发生几年之后，才真正能评价它。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Playboy，但内容明显是乔布斯回答；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What about word processing? You didn't mention that on the list of breakthroughs.
units.translation_cn: 文字处理呢？你没有把它列入突破清单。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气明显像 Playboy 追问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: You're right, I should have listed word processing after VisiCalc. Word processing is the most universally needed application and one of the easiest to understand. It's probably the first use to which most people put their personal computer. There were word processors before personal computers, but a word processor on a personal computer was more of an economic breakthrough, while there was never any form of VisiCalc before the personal computer.
units.translation_cn: 你说得对，我应该把文字处理列在 VisiCalc 之后。文字处理是最普遍需要的应用之一，也是最容易理解的应用之一。它很可能是大多数人使用个人电脑时首先会用到的功能。在个人电脑之前已经有文字处理机，但个人电脑上的文字处理更多是一种经济层面的突破；而在个人电脑之前，从来没有任何形式的 VisiCalc。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Have there been breakthroughs in educational software?
units.translation_cn: 教育软件方面有过突破吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: There has been a lot of very good software in education but not the breakthrough product, not the VisiCalc. I think that will come, but I don't expect it in the next 24 months.
units.translation_cn: 教育领域已经有很多非常好的软件，但还没有出现那个突破性产品，还没有出现教育领域的 VisiCalc。我认为它会出现，但我不期待它会在接下来的 24 个月内出现。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You've stressed the fact that education is a high priority for you. How do you think computers are affecting it?
units.translation_cn: 你一直强调，教育对你来说是很高的优先事项。你认为计算机正在怎样影响教育？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Computers themselves, and software yet to be developed, will revolutionize the way we learn. We formed something called the Apple Education Foundation, and we give several million dollars in cash and equipment to people doing exploratory work with educational software and to schools that can't afford computers. We also wanted Macintosh to become the computer of choice in colleges, just as the Apple II is for grade and high schools. So we looked for six universities that were out to make large-scale commitments to personal computers—by large, meaning more than 1000 apiece—and instead of six, we found 24. We asked the colleges if they would invest at least $2,000,000 each to be part of the Macintosh program. All 24—including the entire Ivy League—did. So in less than a year, Macintosh has become the standard in college computing. I could ship every Macintosh we make this year just to those 24 colleges. We can't, of course, but the demand is there.
units.translation_cn: 计算机本身，以及尚待开发的软件，将会彻底改变我们的学习方式。我们成立了一个叫苹果教育基金会的机构，向那些探索教育软件的人，以及负担不起计算机的学校，提供数百万美元的现金和设备。我们也希望 Macintosh 成为大学里的首选计算机，就像 Apple II 已经成为小学和中学里的首选一样。所以我们寻找了六所准备大规模投入个人电脑的大学——所谓大规模，是指每所学校超过 1000 台。结果我们找到的不是六所，而是 24 所。我们问这些大学，是否愿意各自至少投资 200 万美元，参与 Macintosh 项目。24 所学校全都答应了——包括整个常春藤联盟。所以在不到一年时间里，Macintosh 已经成为大学计算领域的标准。今年我们生产的每一台 Macintosh，我都可以只发给这 24 所大学。当然，我们不能这么做，但需求就在那里。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: But the software isn't there, is it?
units.translation_cn: 但软件还没有到位，对吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Some of it's there. What's not there, the people at colleges are going to write themselves. IBM tried to stop us—I hear it formed a 400-person task force to do it—by giving away IBM PCs. But the colleges were fairly astute. They realized the software investment they were about to embark upon would far outweigh the hardware investment, and they didn't want to spend all that software money on old technology like IBM's. So in many cases, they turned down IBM's offers and went with Macintoshes. In some cases, they used IBM grant money to buy Macintoshes.
units.translation_cn: 其中一部分已经有了。还没有的部分，大学里的人会自己写。IBM 曾试图阻止我们——我听说他们为此组建了一个 400 人的特别工作组——办法是赠送 IBM PC。但这些大学相当精明。它们意识到，自己即将投入的软件投资会远远超过硬件投资，而它们不想把所有软件资金都花在 IBM 那样的旧技术上。所以在很多情况下，它们拒绝了 IBM 的提议，选择了 Macintosh。在有些情况下，它们甚至用 IBM 的资助资金去购买 Macintosh。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Will you name some colleges?
units.translation_cn: 你愿意说出一些大学的名字吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Can't. I'd get them in trouble.
units.translation_cn: 不能说。我会让他们惹上麻烦。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: When you were in college in precomputer days, what did you and your classmates feel was the way to make a contribution? Politics?
units.translation_cn: 在还没有计算机的年代，你上大学时，你和同学们觉得做出贡献的方式是什么？政治吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: None of the really bright people I knew in college went into politics. They all sensed that, in terms of making a change in the world, politics wasn't the place to be in the late Sixties and Seventies. All of them are in business now—which is funny, because they were the same people who trekked off to India or who tried in one way or another to find some sort of truth about life.
units.translation_cn: 我在大学认识的那些真正聪明的人，没有一个进入了政治。他们都感觉到，就改变世界而言，在六十年代末和七十年代，政治并不是应该投入的地方。现在他们全都在商业领域——这很有意思，因为他们正是当年那些远行去印度，或者以这样那样的方式试图寻找某种生命真理的人。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Wasn't business and the lure of money merely the easy choice in the end?
units.translation_cn: 商业和金钱的诱惑，到最后不只是一个容易的选择吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: No, none of those people care about the money. I mean, a lot of them made a lot of money, but they don't really care. Their lifestyles haven't particularly changed. It was the chance to actually try something, to fail, to succeed, to grow. Politics wasn't the place to be these past ten years if you were eager to try things out. As someone who hasn't turned 30 yet, I think your 20s are the time to be impatient, and a lot of these people's idealism would have been deeply frustrated in politics; it would have been blunted. I think it takes a crisis for something to occur in America. And I believe there's going to be a crisis of significant proportions in the early Nineties as these problems our political leaders should have been addressing boil up to the surface. And that's when a lot of these people are going to bring both their practical experience and their idealism into the political realm. You're going to see the best-trained generation ever to go into politics. They're going to know how to choose people, how to get things done, how to lead.
units.translation_cn: 不，这些人没有一个在乎钱。我的意思是，他们中的很多人赚了很多钱，但他们并不真的在乎。他们的生活方式并没有特别改变。真正吸引他们的，是有机会实际去尝试一些事情，去失败，去成功，去成长。如果你渴望尝试事情，那么过去十年里，政治并不是应该去的地方。作为一个还不到 30 岁的人，我认为二十多岁就是应该不耐烦的时候，而这些人的许多理想主义如果放在政治里，会受到深深的挫败，会被磨钝。我认为，美国要发生某些事情，需要一场危机。而我相信，到了九十年代初，会有一场相当规模的危机，因为那些本该由我们的政治领导人处理的问题会浮到表面。到那时，这些人中的很多人会把他们的实践经验和理想主义一起带进政治领域。你会看到有史以来训练最充分的一代人进入政治。他们会知道如何选择人才，如何把事情做成，如何领导。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Doesn't every generation say that?
units.translation_cn: 每一代人不都会这么说吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: These are different times. The technological revolution is more intertwined every day with our economy and our society—more than 50 percent of America's gross national product comes from information-based industries—and most political leaders today have had no background in that revolution. It's going to become crucial that many of the larger decisions we make—how we allot our resources, how we educate our children—be made with an understanding of the technical issues and the directions the technology is taking. And that hasn't begun happening yet. In education, for example, we have close to a national embarrassment. In a society where information and innovation are going to be pivotal, there really is the possibility that America can become a second-rate industrial nation if we lose the technical momentum and leadership we have now.
units.translation_cn: 这是不同的时代。技术革命每天都在与我们的经济和社会更深地交织在一起——美国国民生产总值中超过 50% 来自以信息为基础的行业——而今天的大多数政治领导人并没有这场革命的背景。我们做出的许多重大决定——如何分配资源，如何教育孩子——将变得至关重要，而这些决定必须建立在对技术问题以及技术发展方向的理解之上。但这还没有真正开始发生。比如在教育方面，我们几乎已经到了全国性尴尬的程度。在一个信息和创新将成为关键的社会里，如果美国失去现在拥有的技术动量和领导地位，它确实有可能变成一个二流工业国家。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You mentioned investing in education, but isn't the problem finding the funds in a time of soaring deficits?
units.translation_cn: 你提到了投资教育，但在赤字急剧上升的时候，问题不就是找不到资金吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: We're making the largest investment of capital that humankind has ever made in weapons over the next five years. We have decided, as a society, that that's where we should put our money, and that raises the deficits and, thus, the cost of our capital. Meanwhile, Japan, our nearest competitor on the next technological frontier—the semiconductor industry—has shaped its tax structure, its entire society, toward raising the capital to invest in that area. You get the feeling that connections aren't made in America between things like building weapons and the fact that we might lose our semiconductor industry. We have to educate ourselves to that danger.
units.translation_cn: 未来五年里，我们正在把人类有史以来最大规模的资本投入到武器上。作为一个社会，我们已经决定那就是我们应该投钱的地方，而这会推高赤字，也因此推高我们的资本成本。与此同时，日本，我们在下一个技术前沿——半导体行业——上最接近的竞争者，已经调整了它的税收结构，调整了整个社会，使其能够筹集资本并投入那个领域。你会感觉到，在美国，人们并没有把制造武器这样的事情，和我们可能失去半导体产业这件事联系起来。我们必须让自己认识到这种危险。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: And you think computers will help in that process.
units.translation_cn: 而你认为计算机会帮助这个过程。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Well, I'll tell you a story. I saw a video tape that we weren't supposed to see. It was prepared for the Joint Chiefs of Staff. By watching the tape, we discovered that, at least as of a few years ago, every tactical nuclear weapon in Europe manned by U.S. personnel was targeted by an Apple II computer. Now, we didn't sell computers to the military; they went out and bought them at a dealer's, I guess. But it didn't make us feel good to know that our computers were being used to target nuclear weapons in Europe. The only bright side of it was that at least they weren't [Radio Shack] TRS-80s! Thank God for that. The point is that tools are always going to be used for certain things we don't find personally pleasing. And it's ultimately the wisdom of people, not the tools themselves, that is going to determine whether or not these things are used in positive, productive ways.
units.translation_cn: 嗯，我给你讲个故事。我看过一盘我们本不该看到的录像带。那是为参谋长联席会议准备的。看了那盘录像后，我们发现，至少在几年前，欧洲所有由美国人员操作的战术核武器，都是由 Apple II 电脑来瞄准的。现在，我们并没有把电脑卖给军方；我猜他们是自己去经销商那里买的。但知道我们的电脑被用来瞄准欧洲的核武器，并不会让我们感觉好。唯一值得庆幸的是，至少它们不是 [Radio Shack] TRS-80！感谢上帝。重点在于，工具总是会被用于某些我们个人并不喜欢的事情。最终决定这些东西是否被用在积极、有建设性方向上的，是人的智慧，而不是工具本身。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Where do you see computers and software going in the near future?
units.translation_cn: 你认为计算机和软件在不久的将来会走向哪里？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Thus far, we're pretty much using our computers as good servants. We ask them to do something, we ask them to do some operation like a spread sheet, we ask them to take our key strokes and make a letter out of them, and they do that pretty well. And you'll see more and more perfection of that—computer as servant. But the next thing is going to be computer as guide or agent. And what that means is that it's going to do more in terms of anticipating what we want and doing it for us, noticing connections and patterns in what we do, asking us if this is some sort of generic thing we'd like to do regularly, so that we're going to have, as an example, the concept of triggers. We're going to be able to ask our computers to monitor things for us, and when certain conditions happen, are triggered, the computers will take certain actions and inform us after the fact.
units.translation_cn: 到目前为止，我们基本上是把电脑当作很好的仆人来使用。我们让它们做某件事，让它们执行某种操作，比如电子表格；我们让它们接收我们的键盘输入，并把它们变成一封信，而它们做得相当不错。你会看到这种电脑作为仆人的能力越来越完善。但下一步将会是电脑作为向导或代理人。它的意思是，电脑会更多地预判我们想要什么，并替我们完成它；它会注意到我们行为中的联系和模式，询问我们这是不是某种我们想定期做的通用事项。这样我们就会拥有，比如说，触发器的概念。我们将能够让电脑替我们监控事情；当某些条件发生、被触发时，电脑会采取某些行动，并在事后通知我们。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: For example?
units.translation_cn: 比如说？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Simple things like monitoring your stocks every hour or every day. When a stock gets beyond set limits, the computer will call my broker and electronically sell it and then let me know. Another example is that at the end of the month, the computer will go into the data base and find all the salesmen who exceeded their sales quotas by more than 20 percent and write them a personalized letter from me and send it over the electronic mail system to them, and give me a report on who it sent the letters to each month. There will be a time when our computers have maybe 100 or so of those tasks; they're going to be much more like an agent for us. You're going to see that start to happen a little bit in the next 12 months, but really, it's about three years away. That's the next breakthrough.
units.translation_cn: 一些简单的事情，比如每小时或每天监控你的股票。当某只股票超过设定的上下限时，电脑会打电话给我的经纪人，并以电子方式卖掉它，然后再通知我。另一个例子是，在月底，电脑会进入数据库，找出所有超额完成销售指标 20% 以上的销售员，然后替我给他们写一封个性化的信，通过电子邮件系统发给他们，并每个月给我一份报告，说明它把信发给了哪些人。总有一天，我们的电脑也许会承担 100 个左右这样的任务；它们会更像我们的代理人。你会在接下来 12 个月里开始看到一点点这种事情发生，但真正说起来，它大约还需要三年。这就是下一个突破。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Will we be able to perform all of those things on the hardware we have now? Or are you going to charge us for new machines?
units.translation_cn: 我们能在现在已有的硬件上完成所有这些事情吗？还是你们又要向我们收取新机器的钱？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: All? That would be a dangerous statement, using the word all. I don't know about that. Macintosh was certainly designed with those concepts in mind.
units.translation_cn: 所有？使用所有这个词会是一个危险的说法。我不确定。Macintosh 在设计时当然考虑到了这些概念。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You take great pride in having Apple keep ahead. How do you feel about the older companies that have to play catch-up with the younger companies—or perish?
units.translation_cn: 你很为苹果保持领先而自豪。你怎么看那些较老的公司，它们不得不追赶年轻公司——否则就会消亡？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: That's inevitably what happens. That's why I think death is the most wonderful invention of life. It purges the system of these old models that are obsolete. I think that's one of Apple's challenges, really. When two young people walk in with the next thing, are we going to embrace it and say this is fantastic? Are we going to be willing to drop our models, or are we going to explain it away? I think we'll do better, because we're completely aware of it and we make it a priority.
units.translation_cn: 这就是必然会发生的事。这就是为什么我认为死亡是生命最奇妙的发明。它把系统中那些已经过时的旧模式清除出去。我觉得这其实也是苹果面临的挑战之一。当两个年轻人带着下一个新东西走进来时，我们会拥抱它并说'这太棒了'吗？我们愿意放下自己的模式吗？还是会找理由把它解释掉？我认为我们会做得更好，因为我们完全意识到了这一点，并且把它视为优先事项。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: In thinking about your success, did you ever get to the point where you slapped your head and asked yourself what was happening? After all, it was virtually overnight.
units.translation_cn: 回想你的成功时，你有没有到过某个时刻，拍着脑袋问自己到底发生了什么？毕竟，这几乎是一夜之间发生的。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气明显是 Playboy 提问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I used to think about selling 1,000,000 computers a year, but it was just a thought. When it actually happens, it's a totally different thing. So it was, "Holy shit, it's actually coming true!" But what's hard to explain is that this does not feel like overnight. Next year will be my tenth year. I had never done anything longer than a year in my life. Six months, for me, was a long time when we started Apple. So this has been my life since I've been sort of a free-willed adult. Each year has been so robust with problems and successes and learning experiences and human experiences that a year is a lifetime at Apple. So this has been ten lifetimes.
units.translation_cn: 我以前想过一年卖出 100 万台电脑，但那只是一个想法。当它真的发生时，就是完全不同的事情。所以感觉就是：天哪，它真的要成真了！但很难解释的是，这件事并不像是一夜之间发生的。明年将是我的第十年。我这辈子以前从没有做过任何超过一年的事情。我们刚创办苹果时，对我来说，六个月都是很长的时间。所以从某种意义上说，自从我成为一个有自由意志的成年人以来，这就是我的生活。每一年都充满问题、成功、学习经历和人的经历，在苹果，一年就是一辈子。所以这已经是十辈子了。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Do you know what you want to do with the rest of this lifetime?
units.translation_cn: 你知道自己想用这一生剩下的时间做什么吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: There's an old Hindu saying that comes into my mind occasionally: "For the first 30 years of your life, you make your habits. For the last 30 years of your life, your habits make you." As I'm going to be 30 in February, the thought has crossed my mind.
units.translation_cn: 有一句古老的印度谚语偶尔会浮现在我脑海里：'人生的前 30 年，你塑造自己的习惯；人生的后 30 年，你的习惯塑造你。'因为我二月就要 30 岁了，这个念头确实曾经闪过。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: And?
units.translation_cn: 然后呢？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: And I'm not sure. I'll always stay connected with Apple. I hope that throughout my life I'll sort of have the thread of my life and the thread of Apple weave in and out of each other, like a tapestry. There may be a few years when I'm not there, but I'll always come back. And that's what I may try to do. The key thing to remember about me is that I'm still a student. I'm still in boot camp. If anyone is reading any of my thoughts, I'd keep that in mind. Don't take it all too seriously. If you want to live your life in a creative way, as an artist, you have to not look back too much. You have to be willing to take whatever you've done and whoever you were and throw them away. What are we, anyway? Most of what we think we are is just a collection of likes and dislikes, habits, patterns. At the core of what we are is our values, and what decisions and actions we make reflect those values. That is why it's hard doing interviews and being visible: As you are growing and changing, the more the outside world tries to reinforce an image of you that it thinks you are, the harder it is to continue to be an artist, which is why a lot of times, artists have to go, "Bye. I have to go. I'm going crazy and I'm getting out of here." And they go and hibernate somewhere. Maybe later they re-emerge a little differently.
units.translation_cn: 然后我不确定。我会一直和苹果保持联系。我希望在我一生中，我生命的线索和苹果的线索会像挂毯一样相互穿插、交织。有些年份我也许不在那里，但我总会回来。而这也许就是我会尝试做的事情。关于我，最关键的一点是要记住：我仍然是个学生。我仍然在新兵训练营里。如果有人在读我的这些想法，我会希望他们记住这一点。不要把它们看得太严肃。如果你想以一种创造性的方式生活，像艺术家那样生活，就不能太多回头看。你必须愿意把你已经做过的事情、以及你曾经是谁，都丢掉。我们究竟是什么？我们以为自己是什么，其中大部分只是喜好、厌恶、习惯和模式的集合。我们真正核心的东西是我们的价值观，而我们做出的决定和行动会反映这些价值观。这就是为什么接受采访、变得可见是一件困难的事：当你正在成长和变化时，外部世界越是试图强化它认为你是什么样子的那个形象，你就越难继续做一个艺术家。这就是为什么很多时候，艺术家不得不说：'再见，我得走了。我快疯了，我要离开这里。'然后他们会去某个地方冬眠。也许后来他们会以稍微不同的样子重新出现。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You could take off. You certainly don't have to worry about money. You're still working——
units.translation_cn: 你完全可以离开。你当然不必担心钱。可你还在工作——

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: [Laughs] Because of guilt. Guilt over the money.
units.translation_cn: [笑] 因为内疚。对钱的内疚。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Let's talk about the money. You were a millionaire at 23——
units.translation_cn: 我们来谈谈钱。你 23 岁时就成了百万富翁——
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气明显是 Playboy 提问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: And when I was 24, my net worth was more than $10,000,000; when I was 25, it was more than $100,000,000.
units.translation_cn: 而我 24 岁时，净资产超过了 1000 万美元；25 岁时，超过了 1 亿美元。
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Playboy，但内容明显是乔布斯回答；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What's the main difference between having $1,000,000 and having several hundred million?
units.translation_cn: 拥有 100 万美元和拥有几亿美元，最大的区别是什么？
units.translator_note: 原结构标为 Jobs，但语气明显是 Playboy 提问；暂按原 unit_id 翻译，不改结构。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Visibility. The number of people who have a net worth of more than $1,000,000 in this country is in the tens of thousands. The number of people who have a net worth of more than $10,000,000 gets down to thousands. And the number who have a net worth of more than $100,000,000 gets down to a few hundred.
units.translation_cn: 可见度。在这个国家，净资产超过 100 万美元的人有数万人。净资产超过 1000 万美元的人就降到了几千人。而净资产超过 1 亿美元的人，就降到了几百人。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: What does the money actually mean to you?
units.translation_cn: 钱对你来说实际上意味着什么？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: I still don't understand it. It's a large responsibility to have more than you can spend in your lifetime—and I feel I have to spend it. If you die, you certainly don't want to leave a large amount to your children. It will just ruin their lives. And if you die without kids, it will all go to the Government. Almost everyone would think that he could invest the money back into humanity in a much more astute way than the Government could. The challenges are to figure out how to live with it and to reinvest it back into the world, which means either giving it away or using it to express your concerns or values.
units.translation_cn: 我仍然不理解它。拥有超过你一生所能花掉的钱，是一种很大的责任——而我觉得我必须把它花掉。如果你死了，你当然不会想给孩子留下一大笔钱。那只会毁掉他们的生活。如果你死的时候没有孩子，那钱就都会归政府。几乎每个人都会认为，自己可以比政府更明智地把这些钱重新投入到人类身上。挑战在于弄清楚如何与它共处，并把它重新投入世界。这意味着要么把它捐出去，要么用它来表达你的关切或价值观。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: So what do you do?
units.translation_cn: 那你会怎么做？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: That's a part of my life that I like to keep private. When I have some time, I'm going to start a public foundation. I do some things privately now.
units.translation_cn: 这是我生活中喜欢保持私密的一部分。等我有时间时，我会创办一个公共基金会。现在我私下里做一些事情。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: You could spend all of your time disbursing your money.
units.translation_cn: 你完全可以把所有时间都花在分配你的钱上。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Oh, you have to. I'm convinced that to give away a dollar effectively is harder than to make a dollar.
units.translation_cn: 哦，你必须这么做。我确信，有效地捐出一美元，比赚到一美元更难。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Could that be an excuse to put off doing something?
units.translation_cn: 这会不会成为推迟做点什么的借口？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: No. There are some simple reasons for that. One is that in order to learn how to do something well, you have to fail sometimes. In order to fail, there has to be a measurement system. And that's the problem with most philanthropy—there's no measurement system. You give somebody some money to do something and most of the time you can really never measure whether you failed or succeeded in your judgment of that person or his ideas or their implementation. So if you can't succeed or fail, it's really hard to get better. Also, most of the time, the people who come to you with ideas don't provide the best ideas. You go seek the best ideas out, and that takes a lot of time.
units.translation_cn: 不会。有一些简单的原因。一个原因是，为了学会如何把某件事做好，你有时必须失败。为了能够失败，就必须有一套衡量系统。而这正是大多数慈善事业的问题——没有衡量系统。你给某个人一些钱去做某件事，但大多数时候，你真的永远无法衡量，你对这个人、他的想法或他们的执行判断是失败了还是成功了。所以，如果你无法成功或失败，就很难变得更好。另外，大多数时候，带着想法来找你的人，并不会带来最好的想法。你必须主动去寻找最好的想法，而那需要花很多时间。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: If you plan to use your visibility to create a model for people, why is this one of the areas you choose not to discuss?
units.translation_cn: 如果你打算利用自己的可见度为人们创造一种榜样，为什么这偏偏是你选择不谈的领域之一？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Because I haven't done anything much yet. In that area, actions should speak the loudest.
units.translation_cn: 因为我还没有真正做出多少事情。在那个领域，行动应该说得最响亮。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Are you completely virtuous or do you admit to any extravagances?
units.translation_cn: 你是完全有德行的人，还是承认自己也有一些奢侈之处？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Well, my favorite things in life are books, sushi and…. My favorite things in life don't cost any money. It's really clear that the most precious resource we all have is time. As it is, I pay a price by not having much of a personal life. I don't have the time to pursue love affairs or to tour small towns in Italy and sit in cafes and eat tomato-and-mozzarella salad. Occasionally, I spend a little money to save myself a hassle, which means time. And that's the extent of it. I bought an apartment in New York, but it's because I love that city. I'm trying to educate myself, being from a small town in California, not having grown up with the sophistication and culture of a large city. I consider it part of my education. You know, there are many people at Apple who can buy everything that they could ever possibly want and still have most of their money unspent. I hate talking about this as a problem; people are going to read this and think, Yeah, well, give me your problem. They're going to think I'm an arrogant little asshole.
units.translation_cn: 嗯，我生命中最喜欢的东西是书、寿司和……我生命中最喜欢的东西并不花钱。非常清楚的是，我们所有人拥有的最宝贵资源就是时间。事实上，我因为没有太多私人生活而付出了代价。我没有时间去谈恋爱，或者去意大利的小镇旅行，坐在咖啡馆里吃番茄马苏里拉沙拉。偶尔，我会花一点钱来替自己省掉麻烦，也就是节省时间。差不多也就这样。我在纽约买了一套公寓，但那是因为我爱那座城市。我来自加州一个小地方，没有在大城市的精致和文化中长大，所以我在努力教育自己。我把它看作自己教育的一部分。你知道，苹果有很多人可以买下他们可能想要的一切，却仍然剩下大部分钱。我讨厌把这件事说成一个问题；人们读到这里会想：好啊，那把你的问题给我吧。他们会觉得我是一个傲慢的小混蛋。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: With your wealth and past accomplishments, you have the ability to pursue dreams as few others do. Does that freedom frighten you?
units.translation_cn: 凭借你的财富和过去的成就，你拥有一种很少有人拥有的能力，可以去追求梦想。这种自由会让你害怕吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: The minute you have the means to take responsibility for your own dreams and can be held accountable for whether they come true or not, life is a lot tougher. It's easy to have wonderful thoughts when the chance to implement them is remote. When you've gotten to a place where you at least have a chance of implementing your ideas, there's a lot more responsibility in that.
units.translation_cn: 一旦你拥有了为自己的梦想承担责任的手段，并且可以因为它们是否实现而被追究责任，生活就会艰难得多。当实现它们的机会还很遥远时，拥有美妙的想法很容易。而当你已经到达一个至少有机会实施自己想法的位置时，其中就有了更多责任。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: We've talked about what you see in the near future; what about the far future? If we're still in kindergarten, and you start imagining some of the ways computers are going to change our lives, what do you see?
units.translation_cn: 我们已经谈过你对近期未来的看法；那么更遥远的未来呢？如果我们仍然还在幼儿园阶段，而你开始想象计算机会以哪些方式改变我们的生活，你会看到什么？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: When I came back from India, I found myself asking, What was the one most important thing that had struck me? And I think it was that Western rational thought is not an innate human characteristic. It is a learned ability. It had never occurred to me that if no one taught us how to think this way, we would not think this way. And yet, that's the way it is. Obviously, one of the great challenges of an education is to teach us how to think. What we're finding is that computers are actually going to affect the quality of thinking as more and more of our children have these tools available to them. Humans are tool users. What's really incredible about a book is that you can read what Aristotle wrote. You don't have to have some teacher's interpretation of Aristotle. You can certainly get that, but you can read exactly what Aristotle wrote. That direct transmission of thoughts and ideas is one of the key building blocks of why we are where we are, as a society. But the problem with a book is that you can't ask Aristotle a question. I think one of the potentials of the computer is to somehow … capture the fundamental, underlying principles of an experience.
units.translation_cn: 我从印度回来时，发现自己在问：最触动我的一件最重要的事情是什么？我想，那就是西方理性思维并不是人类与生俱来的特征。它是一种习得的能力。我以前从没想过，如果没有人教我们以这种方式思考，我们就不会以这种方式思考。但事实就是这样。显然，教育的一项巨大挑战，就是教会我们如何思考。我们正在发现，随着越来越多孩子能够使用这些工具，计算机实际上会影响思维的质量。人类是工具使用者。书真正令人难以置信的地方在于，你可以读到亚里士多德写下的东西。你不必接受某位老师对亚里士多德的解释。当然，你也可以获得那种解释，但你可以直接阅读亚里士多德写下的原文。这种思想和观念的直接传递，是我们作为一个社会走到今天的关键基石之一。但书的问题在于，你不能向亚里士多德提问。我认为，计算机的一种潜力，是以某种方式……捕捉一次经验中最根本、最底层的原则。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: For example?
units.translation_cn: 比如说？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Here's a very crude example. The original video game, Pong, captured the principles of gravity, angular momentum and things like that, to where each game obeyed those underlying principles, and yet every game was different—sort of like life. That's the simplest example. And what computer programming can do is to capture the underlying principles, the underlying essence, and then facilitate thousands of experiences based on that perception of the underlying principles. Now, what if we could capture Aristotle's world view—the underlying principles of his world view? Then you could actually ask Aristotle a question. OK. You might say it would not be exactly what Aristotle was. It could be all wrong. But maybe not.
units.translation_cn: 这里有一个非常粗糙的例子。最早的电子游戏 Pong 捕捉了重力、角动量之类的原则，使每一局游戏都遵循那些底层原则，但每一局又都不同——有点像生活。这是最简单的例子。而计算机编程能够做的，是捕捉底层原则、底层本质，然后基于对这些底层原则的理解，促成成千上万种体验。那么，如果我们能够捕捉亚里士多德的世界观——他的世界观中的底层原则——会怎样？那样你实际上就可以问亚里士多德一个问题。好吧，你也许会说，那并不完全是亚里士多德本人。它可能完全错了。但也许未必。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: But you would say it was at least interesting feedback.
units.translation_cn: 但你会说，它至少是有趣的反馈。

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: Exactly. Part of the challenge, I think, is to get these tools to millions and tens of millions of people and to start to refine these tools so that someday we can crudely, and then in a more refined sense, capture an Aristotle or an Einstein or a Land while he's alive. Imagine what that could be like for a young kid growing up. Forget the young kid—for us! And that's part of the challenge.
units.translation_cn: 完全正确。我认为，部分挑战在于，把这些工具送到数百万、数千万人们手中，并开始完善这些工具，这样终有一天，我们能够粗略地，然后以更精细的方式，捕捉一个活着的亚里士多德、或爱因斯坦、或兰德的思想。想象一下这对一个正在成长的孩子来说会是什么样的。忘掉那个孩子——那对我们自己来说也值得想象！这就是挑战的一部分。

units.speaker: Playboy
units.source_text: Will you be working on that yourself?
units.translation_cn: 你会亲自参与这项工作吗？

units.speaker: Jobs
units.source_text: That's for someone else. It's for the next generation. I think an interesting challenge in this area of intellectual inquiry is to grow obsolete gracefully, in the sense that things are changing so fast that certainly by the end of the Eighties, we really want to turn over the reins to the next generation, whose fundamental perceptions are state-of-the-art perceptions, so that they can go on, stand on our shoulders and go much further. It's a very interesting challenge, isn't it? How to grow obsolete with grace.
units.translation_cn: 那是留给别人的。是留给下一代的。我认为，在这个知识探索的领域，有一个有趣的挑战，那就是优雅地变得过时，在这个意义上，事情变化得如此之快，以至于到八十年代末，我们真的想把接力棒交给下一代，让他们的基本认知是最前沿的认知，这样他们可以继续站在我们的肩膀上，走得更远。这是一个非常有趣的挑战，不是吗？如何优雅地变得过时。
